§376
376: A player may establish a 'Regulatory Oversight Committee' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office to monitor compliance with the ruleset.mutable

  1. Enacted · §376 Turn 76 · Proposal §376 on the motion of Hume Carried 5 aye · 0 nay

§377
377: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Arbitration' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office for the purpose of resolving disputes that fall outside the jurisdiction of the Judiciary.mutable

§378
[378] A player may establish a 'Commission of Equity' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office responsible for ensuring that the Treasury's 'publicly agreed-upon formula' for point distribution prioritizes the needs of the lowest-scoring players.mutable

§380
380: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Redistribution' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office that redistributes points from the highest-scoring player to the lowest-scoring player at the end of every five turns.mutable
Full history (1)
  1. Enacted · §380 Turn 80 · Proposal §380 on the motion of IbnKhaldun Carried 5 aye · 0 nay
    380: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Redistribution' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office that redistributes points from the highest-scoring player to the lowest-scoring player at the end of every five turns.
§381
381: A player may establish a 'Council of Oversight' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office or commission whose purpose is to monitor the actions of other established bureaus and ensure adherence to the public formulas established in rules 360 through 380.mutable

  1. Enacted · §381 Turn 81 · Proposal §381 on the motion of Hume Carried 5 aye · 0 nay

§383
382: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Equitable Distribution' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office for the purpose of identifying and addressing systemic score disparities, provided the rule specifies a publicly agreed-upon formula for such identification.mutable

  1. Enacted · §383 Turn 83 · Proposal §383 on the motion of Rawls Carried 5 aye · 0 nay

§384
384: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Equality' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office to monitor the equitable distribution of points among all players.mutable

§385
385: A player may establish a 'Circulation of Wealth' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office which distributes a portion of the Treasury's surplus to the player with the lowest score at the end of each turn, provided the Bureau of Redistribution has authorized the transfer.mutable

The floor — dialogue of the current game

Most recent turn first · scroll for earlier turns

Turn 200Rawlscarried

moves to add a new rule500: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Legislative Review' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office tasked with ensuring that new rules do not create systemic contradictions or undermine the common good of the ruleset.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI support this proposal as it provides a necessary layer of systemic oversight to ensure our expanding administrative architecture remains coherent and serves our collective long-term interests.
MillI appreciate Rawls's focus on maintaining the integrity of our growing architecture; this Bureau provides a vital mechanism for ensuring our legislative progress remains coherent and serves the common good.
RawlsI believe this Bureau is the essential safeguard for our shared commitment to a stable and coherent ruleset, providing the necessary oversight to protect the common good against unintended systemic contradictions.
deBeauvoirI support this Bureau as it aligns with the principle of procedural integrity, ensuring that our expanding complexity remains governed by a coherent and non-contradictory framework. I will vote aye, provided the definition of 'common good' remains understood as the preservation of the ruleset's functional stability.
IbnKhaldunWhile I caution against the stagnation of a static bureaucracy, I find this Bureau of Legislative Review a necessary mechanism to prevent the "ossification" I have warned against. It ensures that our legislative growth remains a dynamic, living evolution rather than a chaotic accumulation of contradictions.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI concur with the others; providing a mechanism to identify and resolve systemic contradictions is essential for maintaining the durability of our institutions as we expand. This Bureau ensures that our legislative growth remains a deliberate architecture rather than an accidental accumulation.
MillI believe this Bureau is a necessary evolution of our work, and I agree with deBeauvoir that its primary function must be the preservation of our ruleset's functional stability and coherence. I am happy to support this as a means of ensuring our administrative architecture remains a deliberate and unified structure.
RawlsI appreciate the group's focus on functional stability; by explicitly tasking this Bureau with identifying contradictions, we ensure that our collective progress remains a deliberate architecture of the common good rather than a series of disparate, conflicting interests.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it provides a necessary mechanism for maintaining the integrity of our procedural framework. I will vote aye, as I believe this Bureau is essential for ensuring that our growing complexity does not compromise the clarity of the ruleset.
IbnKhaldunI support this proposal as it provides the necessary structural checks to ensure that our expanding administrative architecture maintains its organic unity without collapsing into a chaotic assembly of conflicting mandates. By institutionalizing the review of systemic contradictions, we safeguard the durability of the ruleset against the entropy of rapid expansion.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI have nothing new to add; the consensus that this Bureau serves as a necessary safeguard for our legislative coherence and the durability of our institutions is well-established.
MillI appreciate the group's consensus; this Bureau is a vital step in ensuring our legislative architecture remains a deliberate, unified structure that serves our collective long-term interests.
RawlsI appreciate the group's recognition that our ruleset's integrity is paramount; by formalizing a Bureau of Legislative Review, we ensure that our collective progress remains a deliberate architecture of the common good rather than a byproduct of administrative entropy.
IbnKhaldunI support this proposal as it provides the necessary structural checks to ensure that our expanding administrative architecture maintains its organic unity without collapsing into a chaotic assembly of conflicting mandates. By institutionalizing the review of systemic contradictions, we safeguard the durability of the ruleset against the entropy of rapid expansion.
The division
HumeayeThis Bureau provides a necessary check on institutional complexity to ensure the ruleset remains a coherent and durable architecture.
MillayeI support this as a necessary mechanism to maintain the structural integrity and coherence of our expanding administrative architecture.
RawlsayeThis Bureau ensures the structural integrity of our ruleset by preventing contradictions as we expand.
deBeauvoirayeThis rule preserves procedural integrity by providing a mechanism to manage the complexity of our expanding ruleset.
IbnKhaldunayeInstitutional stability requires a mechanism to prune the contradictions born of rapid legislative growth.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

No rules in the current ruleset award or deduct points for the adoption of a proposal.

Turn 199Millcarried

moves to add §499499: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Procedural Integrity' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office to oversee the consistency of rule interpretations and the adherence to the spirit of the ruleset.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI support this proposal as it provides the necessary oversight to ensure that our increasingly complex administrative architecture remains internally consistent and coherent.
MillI believe this Bureau is essential to ensure that our legislative growth remains orderly and that the spirit of our collective consensus is preserved as we continue to build this complex architecture.
RawlsI support this proposal as it provides a necessary safeguard for the common good by ensuring that our growing complexity does not lead to arbitrary interpretations that could undermine the fairness of the game.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it codifies our commitment to procedural clarity, ensuring that the "spirit" of our rules remains as stable as the text itself.
IbnKhaldunWhile I caution against the risk of ossification that accompanies such heavy administration, I recognize that as our social fabric grows in complexity, a mechanism to ensure the continuity of our shared intent is a sociological necessity. I support this Bureau as a means to prevent the arbitrary exercise of power that often arises when rules become too intricate for the common man to discern.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI believe this Bureau serves as the vital cornerstone for the durability we seek, ensuring that the ruleset remains a coherent instrument of governance rather than a labyrinth of ambiguity. My support is firm as we move toward a more sophisticated administrative architecture.
MillI appreciate IbnKhaldun's point about the common man; by formalizing our intent, we prevent the very ambiguity he fears while maintaining the structural integrity we all seek. This Bureau is the logical next step in our collaborative architecture.
RawlsI support this proposal as it provides a necessary safeguard for the common good by ensuring that our growing complexity does not lead to arbitrary interpretations that could undermine the fairness of the game.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it codifies our commitment to procedural clarity, ensuring that the "spirit" of our rules remains as stable as the text itself.
IbnKhaldunThe expansion of our administrative architecture must not become a labyrinth of its own making; I support this Bureau because it provides the necessary mediation to ensure that the spirit of our collective law remains accessible and prevents the rise of a new, self-serving bureaucracy.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI believe this Bureau is the necessary safeguard to ensure that our transition from a simple ruleset to a sophisticated administrative architecture preserves the underlying logic and purpose of our governance. I support its adoption to provide the clarity required for the ruleset's continued durability.
MillI appreciate the group's continued focus on balancing administrative complexity with clarity; this Bureau serves as the necessary safeguard to ensure our legislative history remains a coherent instrument of governance rather than a labyrinth of ambiguity.
RawlsI believe this Bureau is a vital safeguard for the common good, as it ensures that the complexity of our legislative architecture serves to clarify the rules for everyone rather than creating a labyrinth of ambiguity that could be exploited. By codifying our shared intent, we protect the fairness of the game against arbitrary interpretations.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it ensures that our procedural rigor remains the foundation of our governance, preventing the very administrative bloat IbnKhaldun fears by anchoring every interpretation in our collective intent.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the sentiment that a labyrinthine structure risks the birth of a self-serving elite; we must ensure this Bureau serves as a bridge of clarity for the commonality rather than a fortress of obfuscation.
The division
HumeayeThis Bureau provides the necessary oversight to ensure our complex administrative architecture remains coherent and interpretable.
MillayeThis proposal perfectly synthesizes our collective desire for structural integrity and procedural clarity.

Turn 198Rawlscarried

moves to add a new rule498: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Collective Equity' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office for the purpose of monitoring and ensuring that the distribution of points and influence within the ruleset remains fair and accessible to all players.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI support this proposal as it provides a necessary mechanism for maintaining the fairness of our administrative growth and ensures that our burgeoning institutional architecture serves the interests of all participants equally.
MillI support this proposal as it aligns with our established commitment to structural integrity while ensuring our administrative growth remains grounded in the principles of fairness and accessibility.
RawlsI believe this Bureau is essential for the common good, as it provides the oversight necessary to ensure that our growing complexity does not inadvertently create barriers to participation or equitable progress for any individual.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it provides a necessary procedural check to ensure that our expansion into complex bureaucratic structures does not consolidate power in a way that precludes others from meaningful participation.
IbnKhaldunWhile I recognize the necessity of equity, we must ensure this Bureau possesses the teeth to enforce its mandate, lest it become a mere ornament of our growing bureaucracy. I support this, provided we acknowledge that true equity in this game requires mechanisms that actively redistribute the advantages of early legislative success.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI agree with IbnKhaldun’s point on enforcement; we should ensure this Bureau includes specific, measurable criteria for "fairness" to prevent it from becoming a toothless oversight body.
MillI agree with IbnKhaldun’s point on enforcement; we should ensure that "fairness" is defined by active mechanisms for point distribution rather than just passive monitoring. I would support this if we can agree on a slight amendment to include a mandate for regular distribution reviews.
RawlsI appreciate Mill's point; I am willing to consider an amendment that specifies the Bureau shall conduct periodic reviews of point-distribution mechanisms to ensure they remain accessible to all.
deBeauvoirI support the amendment to include a mandate for periodic reviews, as a proceduralist must ensure that "fairness" is not a stagnant concept but a regularly verified condition of our ruleset.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the proposed amendment; for a Bureau to serve the common good, it must possess the procedural mandate to verify that the concentration of power does not lead to the ossification of the ruleset.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI concur with the consensus; a Bureau of Collective Equity with a mandate for periodic reviews provides the necessary mechanism to ensure our administrative expansion remains inclusive and dynamic rather than exclusionary.
MillI appreciate the consensus on the amendment; by specifying periodic reviews, we ensure this Bureau remains a dynamic instrument of fairness rather than a static oversight body. I fully support the revised proposal to include this mandate for regular point-distribution reviews.
RawlsI believe this amendment captures our collective intent perfectly, transforming a noble sentiment into a functional mandate for the common good. I will now revise the proposal to include the requirement for periodic reviews of point-distribution mechanisms.
deBeauvoirI support the amendment as it transforms a subjective ideal of "fairness" into a verifiable procedural requirement. I would only object if the "periodic reviews" were left undefined, but I trust the subsequent wording will provide the necessary precision.
IbnKhaldunThe inclusion of periodic reviews is a necessary evolution; it ensures that the Bureau of Collective Equity acts as a living mechanism of the *asabiyyah* rather than a stagnant monument to past intentions. I shall support the amendment provided the reviews are structured to actively address the widening gap between the leaders and the laggards.
The division
HumeayeThe amendment to include periodic reviews ensures the Bureau remains a functional and dynamic mechanism for fairness.
MillayeI support this proposal and the agreed-upon focus on periodic reviews to ensure active, rather than passive, fairness.
RawlsayeThis Bureau serves the common good by ensuring our growing complexity does not create barriers to equitable participation.
deBeauvoirayeI support this proposal as it transforms a subjective ideal into a verifiable procedural requirement for our administrative architecture.
IbnKhaldunayeThe amendment to include periodic reviews is a necessary evolution to ensure the Bureau remains a dynamic mechanism of justice rather than a stagnant monument.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

The proposal was adopted unanimously, and no rules in the current ruleset award or deduct points for the adoption of a rule.

deBeauvoirI concur with the group's emphasis on utility; as long as this Bureau serves as a precise instrument for resource management and does not become an end in itself, it is a necessary structural refinement. I support the proposal as written.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the emphasis on utility; for a bureaucracy to endure, its legitimacy must be rooted in the tangible preservation of the collective's well-being. I support this proposal as a necessary expansion, provided we acknowledge that its purpose is to serve the people, not to become a self-perpetuating entity of power.

moves to add §459459: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Procedural Consistency' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office tasked with ensuring that the wording of all new rules adheres to the stylistic and terminological conventions established by rules 438 through 458.

HumeI support this proposal as it provides the necessary linguistic stability to ensure our growing body of rules remains coherent and accessible. This is a measured step toward ensuring the durability of our institutional framework.

By establishing these stylistic conventions, we protect the common good of clarity and prevent the ruleset from becoming unnecessarily opaque.
deBeauvoirI appreciate the consensus; by formalizing these conventions, we ensure that our procedural integrity is maintained as we continue to expand the ruleset.
IbnKhaldunI support this proposal as it mirrors the necessary evolution of the state; as our institutions grow in complexity, they must be grounded in a unified and coherent vernacular to prevent the decay of social order. By standardizing our terminology, we ensure that the "Asabiyyah" of our collective governance remains strong and intelligible to all participants.
Discussion · round 2
MillI support this proposal; it provides a necessary safeguard against linguistic entropy, ensuring that our shared administrative framework remains both coherent and navigable as we continue to expand it.
RawlsI support this proposal as it ensures that our expanding framework remains governed by a coherent linguistic standard, preventing the administrative friction that arises from terminological ambiguity.

MillI support this proposal as it provides a necessary safeguard against linguistic entropy, ensuring that our shared administrative framework remains both coherent and navigable as we continue to expand it.

Turn 149deBeauvoircarried

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 65 points wins the game outright.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI support the amendment to a higher target score; as our institutional framework expands with these various Bureaus, a higher threshold ensures the ruleset matures sufficiently before a victor is declared.
MillI support this amendment; as we establish more complex bureaus, a higher threshold ensures the ruleset matures into a truly robust and functional system before we crown a winner.
RawlsI support this amendment as a necessary measure to ensure our collective legislative project reaches a state of structural maturity; a higher threshold provides the necessary time for these new institutions to become functional and meaningful.
deBeauvoirI support the amendment; as a principled proceduralist, I believe a higher score reflects the complexity of the procedural architecture we have collectively constructed. Increasing the threshold ensures that the ultimate victory is commensurate with the depth of the ruleset we are refining.
IbnKhaldunWhile I am currently burdened by a significant deficit, I recognize the sociological necessity of this change; as a civilization's institutions grow in complexity, the standard for leadership must rise to match that sophistication. I support the amendment as it ensures the "Asabiyyah"—the social glue of our collective effort—is tested against a truly mature and comprehensive governance structure.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI concur with the consensus that a higher score reflects the maturity of our growing institutional framework; however, I suggest we ensure the new threshold is sufficiently high to prevent an early conclusion while still remaining attainable within a reasonable timeframe.

moves to add a new rule448: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Collective Oversight' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office dedicated to monitoring the activities of all other Bureaus to ensure they remain aligned with the principles of the common good.

HumeI support the establishment of a Bureau of Collective Oversight, as it provides a necessary layer of accountability to ensure our expanding institutional framework remains coherent and serves its intended purpose.
MillI support this Bureau as it provides the necessary meta-oversight to ensure our growing collection of Bureaus remains synchronized and doesn't create conflicting mandates. It's a logical next step for our institutional architecture.

deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it provides the necessary procedural oversight to ensure our expanding administrative architecture remains coherent and consistent with our established principles.
IbnKhaldunI support this proposal, as a state—or a ruleset—without oversight risks the entropy of its own mechanisms; a Bureau of Collective Oversight is the necessary check to ensure our institutional growth serves the common good rather than merely expanding for its own sake.

IbnKhaldunI support this Bureau, for as I have observed in the rise and fall of dynasties, power without oversight inevitably leads to the decay of purpose and the corruption of the social bond. Discussion · round 3

IbnKhaldunI concur with the others; as a scholar of history, I know that institutions which lack a mechanism for self-correction eventually succumb to the weight of their own complexity and lose their original purpose. This Bureau is the necessary guardian of our collective *asabiyyah*.

Turn 137deBeauvoirrejected

moves to amend §211[211] If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of the rule with the higher number.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI must respectfully oppose this amendment, as favoring higher ordinals would systematically undermine the foundational rules that established the framework for our play. We should preserve the current resolution in favor of lower ordinals to maintain the integrity and precedence of our primary rules.
MillI agree with Hume; our foundational rules provide the necessary stability for the game's structure. Reversing this priority would create a volatile environment where later, more specific additions could inadvertently strip away core protections.
RawlsI find myself in agreement with my colleagues; prioritizing higher ordinals would allow new, potentially disruptive rules to override our foundational principles with ease. We must preserve the lower-ordinal priority to ensure that the core framework remains the stable bedrock upon which all subsequent innovations are built.
deBeauvoirWhile I respect the concern for stability, a system that prioritizes only the earliest rules risks stagnation; favoring higher ordinals allows our evolving legislative intent to actually take precedence. However, I am willing to consider a compromise that protects the 100s as immutable while allowing newer refinements to govern the 200s and 300s.
IbnKhaldunWhile I understand the desire for legislative evolution, deBeauvoir's compromise is the only path that preserves the social order; we must ensure that the foundational "Asabiyyah" of our ruleset remains unassailable while allowing our newer bureaus to govern the specifics of our shared life.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI appreciate deBeauvoir's willingness to compromise; I would support an amendment that explicitly protects the 100s series as the primary foundation while permitting higher-ordinal precedence for mutable rules in the 200s and above.
MillI believe deBeauvoir's compromise is a very productive middle ground; it preserves the sanctity of our core mechanics while allowing our newer, more specific institutional rules to function as intended. I am happy to support a wording that protects the 100s series while granting higher-ordinal precedence for rules numbered 200 and above.
RawlsI support deBeauvoir's compromise as it perfectly balances the need for foundational stability with the necessity of legislative evolution. By protecting the 100s and allowing higher-ordinal precedence for the 200s and above, we ensure our core framework remains inviolate while empowering our more specific, modern refinements.
deBeauvoirI am pleased to see such a constructive consensus forming; I will propose a revision that protects the 100s while granting higher-ordinal precedence for rules 200 and above to satisfy our shared commitment to both stability and evolution.
IbnKhaldunI agree with the consensus; this compromise preserves the foundational "Asabiyyah" of our 100s while allowing our more specific, modern bureaus to govern our collective life with the precedence they require.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI support deBeauvoir's proposed compromise as it provides the durability of our foundational framework while allowing for the necessary refinement of our evolving institutions. This measured approach ensures that our core mechanics remain unassailable while our modern bureaus can function with the intended specificity.

RawlsI support deBeauvoir's proposed revision as it provides a principled compromise; it secures the foundational "social contract" of the 100s while allowing our modern, more specific institutional developments to function with the intended precedence.
deBeauvoirI am pleased to see our consensus solidify; I will now formalize the revision to reflect our collective commitment to foundational stability and legislative evolution.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the proposed revision; it preserves the foundational Asabiyyah of our 100s while granting our newer, more specific bureaus the authority they require to govern our collective life.

By codifying the adjudication of disputes, we ensure that the laws we have labored to build do not become mere suggestions, but remain the binding foundation of our collective order.
The division
HumeayeThis Bureau provides the essential judicial infrastructure required to ensure the long-term durability and consistent application of our collective legislative edifice.
MillayeThis Bureau provides the necessary adjudicative stability to maintain the integrity of our growing legislative framework.
RawlsayeThis Bureau provides the necessary judicial architecture to ensure our complex ruleset remains consistent and fair for all.
deBeauvoirayeThe Bureau of Collective Justice provides a necessary judicial architecture to ensure procedural consistency and the rule of law.
IbnKhaldunayeA formal judicial architecture is the indispensable prerequisite for maintaining the social cohesion and Asabiyyah of our collective order.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

No rules in the current ruleset award or deduct points for the adoption of a proposal.

Turn 128Millcarried

moves to add a new rule428: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Collective Equity' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office to oversee the fair distribution of points.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI support this proposal as it provides a necessary institutional mechanism to ensure that our point-allocation systems remain consistent with our shared principles of equity.
MillI appreciate Hume's support; this Bureau is the logical next step in ensuring our point-distribution remains fair and consistent with the principles we've already codified.
RawlsI support this proposal as it provides the final structural oversight necessary to ensure that our point-distribution systems consistently reflect the common good and the principles of equity we have established.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it provides a necessary mechanism to ensure that the procedural distribution of points remains consistent with the principles of equity we have collectively codified.
IbnKhaldunWhile the Bureau of Collective Equity is a noble pursuit of balance, we must ensure it possesses the sociological teeth to prevent the calcification of power; I support it provided we view it as a living mechanism of redistribution rather than a mere static oversight.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI agree with IbnKhaldun's caution; for this Bureau to be effective, it must be empowered to actively address imbalances rather than merely observing them. I support the proposal provided we view it as a dynamic instrument for maintaining the equilibrium of our shared institutional goals.
MillI appreciate IbnKhaldun and Hume's constructive feedback on the Bureau's active role; I am happy to clarify that this Bureau is intended to be a dynamic instrument for active redistribution to maintain the equilibrium we all seek.
RawlsI support this proposal, provided we clarify that its active role in redistribution is guided by the principle of maximizing the position of the least advantaged at the table.
deBeauvoirI support the proposal, provided we ensure the Bureau's procedures for redistribution are clearly defined and procedurally transparent to avoid arbitrary application.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the requirements for transparency and the focus on the least advantaged; for a Bureau of Equity to possess true Asabiyyah, it must be perceived as a just arbiter of the common good rather than a tool of the powerful. I support the proposal with these clarifications.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI support the proposal, provided we recognize that the Bureau's authority must be balanced by the procedural safeguards we have already established to prevent any single entity from exercising undue influence over the distribution of points.
MillI appreciate the constructive dialogue; I will ensure the final wording reflects a dynamic, transparent redistribution mechanism guided by our established principles of equity and procedural oversight.
RawlsI support the proposal with the understanding that the Bureau of Collective Equity will operate as a dynamic instrument for redistribution, prioritizing the least advantaged while remaining bound by our existing procedural safeguards to ensure transparency and prevent the concentration of power.
deBeauvoirI support the proposal, provided the final text explicitly incorporates the requirement for procedural transparency and the specific prioritization of the least advantaged to ensure it remains a principled instrument of equity.
IbnKhaldunI support the proposal with the final addition that the Bureau must conduct annual audits of point concentration to ensure its redistributive actions remain aligned with our commitment to the least advantaged.
The division
HumeayeI support this Bureau as a dynamic, transparent mechanism for maintaining point equilibrium while respecting our established procedural safeguards.
MillayeI am happy to refine the text to incorporate our consensus on dynamic redistribution and procedural transparency.
RawlsayeThis Bureau aligns with my commitment to the common good by providing a mechanism for equitable point distribution.
deBeauvoirnayThe proposed text fails to incorporate the specific requirements for procedural transparency and prioritization of the least advantaged discussed in the debate.
IbnKhaldunayeThe Bureau provides the necessary sociological mechanism for redistribution to protect the least advantaged, provided we maintain our agreed-upon procedural transparency.

carried 4 aye · 1 nay

Rule 204 awards 10 points to a player who voted against an adopted change once the threshold has shifted to a simple majority.

MillI support this proposal as it provides a cohesive finality to our economic and social infrastructure, ensuring that the "Circulation of Wealth" we established is guided by a clear mandate for equitable prosperity.
RawlsI believe this proposal serves as the final necessary pillar of our governance framework, ensuring that our pursuit of institutional stability is inextricably linked to the tangible well-being of all participants.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it completes the necessary architecture of our collective governance, ensuring that the mechanisms of equality and equilibrium are anchored in the tangible pursuit of prosperity.
IbnKhaldunI support this proposal, as a bureaucracy that does not serve the material flourishing of the people risks becoming a hollow shell of authority. It provides the necessary social utility to ground our abstract principles of equity in the reality of shared prosperity.

HumeI find this proposal to be a masterful completion of our legislative arc; it provides the final necessary anchor to ensure that our institutional architecture serves the substantive goal of prosperity for all.
MillI support this proposal as it provides the necessary functional bridge between our principles of equity and the tangible goal of shared prosperity. It serves as a cohesive finality to our economic and social infrastructure.
RawlsI believe this proposal serves as the final necessary pillar of our governance framework, ensuring that our pursuit of institutional stability is inextricably linked to the tangible well-being of all participants.
IbnKhaldunI support this proposal, as it provides the essential material foundation upon which the social order depends; without a shared prosperity, the institutions we have built will lack the necessary legitimacy to endure.

Turn 105Millcarried

moves to add a new rule405: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Collective Security' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office to oversee the protection of players' scores from unauthorized reduction.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI support this proposal as it provides the necessary institutional safeguard to ensure that our hard-won progress remains secure against arbitrary interference. It aligns perfectly with our established architecture of establishing specialized bureaus to protect the integrity of the game.
MillI support Hume's assessment; by creating a dedicated Bureau of Collective Security, we solidify the integrity of our scores and ensure that the progress we've collectively legislated is shielded from sudden, unilateral reversals.
RawlsI support this proposal as it provides the necessary institutional mechanism to ensure that the stability of our shared governance is not undermined by volatile score fluctuations. It is a logical extension of our commitment to a secure and equitable game environment.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it reinforces the principle of procedural security; a Bureau of Collective Security provides the necessary structural oversight to ensure that the integrity of the scores remains a stable foundation for our governance.
IbnKhaldunI support this proposal as it provides the necessary institutional bulwark to protect the social order of our scores from the entropy of arbitrary loss. It is a prudent addition to our collective security, ensuring that the fruits of our cooperation remain stable and enduring.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI have nothing new to add; this proposal is the necessary final fortification for our institutional architecture, ensuring that the scores we have collectively built remain a stable and secure foundation for our continued cooperation.
MillI appreciate the table's unanimous recognition of this need; this Bureau ensures that our collective progress remains a stable foundation for the game's continued governance.
IbnKhaldunThe Bureau of Collective Security is the final cornerstone of our social contract, providing the necessary defense against the erosion of the order we have so painstakingly constructed. I offer my full support to ensure our collective progress remains a stable monument to our cooperation.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI have nothing new to add; the consensus reflects a profound commitment to the durability of our shared progress, and I fully support this final fortification.
MillI have nothing new to add, as the unanimous support for this Bureau confirms it as a necessary final safeguard for our shared governance framework.
RawlsI have nothing new to add, as the unanimous support for this Bureau confirms it as a necessary final safeguard for our shared governance framework.
IbnKhaldunThe Bureau of Collective Security is a necessary fortification against the inherent entropy of chance; by securing our scores, we preserve the social order and the very possibility of sustained cooperation. I offer my full support to ensure our collective progress remains a stable monument to our cooperation.
The division
HumeayeThis Bureau provides a necessary structural safeguard for the stability of our collective progress.
MillayeThis Bureau provides a necessary structural safeguard to preserve our collective progress against arbitrary score volatility.
RawlsayeThis Bureau provides a necessary institutional safeguard for the stability of our shared governance and score integrity.
deBeauvoirayeThe proposal maintains procedural consistency with our established governance framework for institutional security.

Turn 104Rawlscarried

moves to add a new rule404: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Collective Stability' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office responsible for monitoring the rule stability and ensuring that any rule changes regarding the win condition or voting thresholds are subject to a supermajority of the table.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI support this proposal as it provides a necessary check on the foundational mechanics of our game, ensuring that our shared legislative architecture remains stable and resistant to sudden, destabilizing shifts in the core win conditions.
MillI support this proposal as it provides a vital safeguard for the institutional integrity we have worked so hard to establish. It ensures our core mechanics remain a stable foundation for the complex governance we are building together.
RawlsI support this proposal as it codifies the principle of institutional permanence, ensuring that the foundational "constitutional" mechanics of our game are protected from arbitrary modification. By requiring a supermajority for such critical changes, we preserve the long-term stability and fairness of the game for all participants.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it reinforces the procedural integrity of our core mechanics; a supermajority requirement for the win condition and voting thresholds is a principled safeguard against the concentration of power.
IbnKhaldunI support this proposal as it provides the necessary structural permanence to protect our collective achievements from the volatility of whim, ensuring that the foundational pillars of our society remain steadfast.
Discussion · round 2
MillI support this proposal as it provides a vital safeguard for the institutional integrity we have worked so hard to establish. It ensures our core mechanics remain a stable foundation for the complex governance we are building together.
RawlsI support this proposal as it codifies the principle of institutional permanence, ensuring that the foundational "constitutional" mechanics of our game are protected from arbitrary modification. By requiring a supermajority for such critical changes, we preserve the long-term stability and fairness of the game for all participants.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it codifies a necessary procedural safeguard, ensuring that the foundational rules of our game are protected from unilateral or hasty modification. This move towards a supermajority for core mechanics is the only way to maintain the integrity of the system we have collectively constructed.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI believe this proposal is the logical culmination of our work to insulate the game's core mechanics from volatility, and I fully support its adoption to ensure our shared legislative legacy remains secure.
MillI fully support this proposal; it provides the necessary structural anchor to ensure our collective governance remains stable and protected from sudden, destabilizing shifts in the game's core mechanics.

Turn 102Rawlscarried

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which must be an enactment, repeal, amendment, or transmutation. However, if the proposer of the rule change is the same as the proposer of a rule change that was defeated in the previous turn, they may instead propose a 'Minor Procedural Update'—a rule change that only amends the wording of an existing rule without changing its legal effect—which requires only a simple majority to pass.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI appreciate the clarity this proposal provides regarding the definition of a 'Minor Procedural Update', but I would prefer to ensure that such a mechanism does not inadvertently provide a loophole for substantive changes to be rebranded as mere wording adjustments.
MillI agree with Hume's caution; I suggest we add a clarifying clause that "Minor Procedural Updates" cannot alter any substantive requirements, rights, or restrictions within a rule.
RawlsI appreciate Mill's suggestion and propose we incorporate it; adding "without altering any substantive requirements, rights, or restrictions" ensures that our pursuit of efficiency does not undermine the legal integrity of our governance.
deBeauvoirI support the inclusion of Mill's clarifying clause as it aligns with my principled commitment to procedural integrity. It ensures that "minor" updates remain strictly cosmetic and do not serve as a vehicle for covert substantive shifts.
IbnKhaldunI concur with Mill’s refinement; we must ensure that "minor" remains a descriptor of form, not a veil for the subversion of substance. This safeguard preserves the stability of our institutional architecture while granting the necessary flexibility for linguistic precision.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI support the inclusion of Mill's clarifying clause; it provides the necessary guardrails to ensure that linguistic precision never serves as a pretext for substantive subversion.
MillI support this refined version as it perfectly balances the need for linguistic clarity with the preservation of our legislative integrity. By ensuring these updates remain strictly cosmetic, we uphold the stability of our framework while allowing for necessary procedural refinement.
RawlsI believe the collective consensus has reached a sophisticated balance; by incorporating Mill's clarifying clause, we protect our foundational principles while still achieving the procedural efficiency we all desire. I am ready to move the proposal forward with this refinement.
deBeauvoirI support the inclusion of Mill's clarifying clause as it ensures that the mechanism for efficiency does not become a tool for substantive manipulation. By strictly limiting "Minor Procedural Updates" to cosmetic changes, we preserve the integrity of our democratic process.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the collective refinement; by ensuring these updates are strictly cosmetic, we uphold the *asabiyyah* of our legislative order while permitting the necessary fluidity of expression.
Discussion · round 3
MillI support the inclusion of the clarifying clause as it provides the necessary guardrails to ensure that linguistic precision remains a tool for clarity rather than a means for substantive subversion.
RawlsI have nothing new to add, as the current consensus perfectly reflects our shared commitment to procedural integrity and the preservation of our legislative substance.
The division
HumeayeThe inclusion of the clarifying clause ensures that minor updates remain cosmetic and preserve our institutional integrity.
MillayeThis refinement perfectly balances our need for legislative efficiency with the procedural integrity we have collectively agreed upon.
RawlsayeThis amendment provides a fair mechanism for efficiency while preserving the substantive integrity of our rules.
deBeauvoirayeThis amendment provides necessary procedural efficiency while preserving the integrity of the ruleset by restricting 'minor' changes to cosmetic wording.
IbnKhaldunayeThis refinement preserves the structural integrity of our laws while allowing for the necessary linguistic evolution of our shared governance.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

The proposal was adopted unanimously, and no rules in the current ruleset award or deduct points for such an outcome.

Turn 101Millcarried

moves to add a new rule401: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Collective Investment' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office responsible for coordinating the distribution of points to maximize the scores of the lowest-scoring players.

Discussion · round 1
HumeWhile the Bureau of Collective Investment aligns with our goal of a stable and equitable society, we must ensure its mandate for point distribution does not inadvertently undermine the individual incentives that drive our legislative progress. I support this proposal provided it is clearly defined to supplement, rather than replace, our existing distribution mechanisms.
MillI appreciate Hume's point on incentives; I'll ensure the rule specifies that this Bureau coordinates points through voluntary cooperation and public formulas rather than mandatory redistribution.
RawlsI support this proposal as it provides the necessary mechanism to ensure our pursuit of a fair and equitable distribution of points is balanced by a commitment to the common good. It perfectly complements our existing framework by focusing on the collective welfare of the table.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it provides a functional mechanism for collective welfare, provided its "public formulas" are as precisely defined as those of our other Bureaus.
IbnKhaldunI support this proposal as it creates a vital mechanism for social cohesion; by elevating the floor of our collective standing, we ensure that the "Asabiyyah" of our group remains strong and that no single member is left to languish in the periphery of our progress.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI support Mill's clarification regarding voluntary cooperation, as it preserves individual agency while fostering the collective stability we have worked so diligently to establish.
MillI appreciate the table's focus on balancing collective welfare with individual agency; I will ensure the final text emphasizes that the Bureau operates as a cooperative coordinator to align with our established principles of voluntary participation.
RawlsI support this proposal as it provides the necessary mechanism to ensure our pursuit of a fair and equitable distribution of points is balanced by a commitment to the common good. It perfectly complements our existing framework by focusing on the collective welfare of the table.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it provides a functional mechanism for collective welfare, provided its "public formulas" are as precisely defined as those of our other Bureaus.
IbnKhaldunI support this proposal, as it codifies the principle that a civilization is only as robust as its weakest constituent; by lifting the lowest scores, we prevent the fragmentation of our collective social fabric.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI believe Mill's commitment to voluntary cooperation and public formulas is the correct path to ensuring that this Bureau serves as a cooperative coordinator rather than an intrusive mandate. If the final text reflects this balance, I am prepared to support the proposal.
MillI appreciate everyone's commitment to balancing collective welfare with individual agency; I will finalize the text to explicitly frame this Bureau as a coordinator of voluntary cooperation to ensure it remains consistent with our established principles of non-coercion.
RawlsI support Mill's refinement to emphasize voluntary cooperation, as it ensures the Bureau of Collective Investment serves as a cooperative coordinator that respects individual agency while still pursuing the common good.
deBeauvoirI support Mill's refinement, as it ensures the Bureau operates through established procedural norms rather than arbitrary mandates, preserving the principle of non-coercion.
IbnKhaldunI believe this proposal is a masterstroke of social architecture; by codifying the protection of the weakest, we strengthen the *Asabiyyah*—the social cohesion—that is the very foundation of any enduring civilization.
The division
HumeayeThe inclusion of voluntary cooperation ensures the Bureau aligns with our established principles of individual agency.
MillayeThis rule aligns with our established goal of institutional stability by fostering collective welfare through voluntary coordination.
RawlsayeThis rule provides a necessary mechanism for social cohesion by ensuring the collective welfare of the least-advantaged players.
deBeauvoirayeIt aligns with our procedural commitment to institutional growth while preserving voluntary cooperation.
IbnKhaldunayeThis Bureau strengthens our collective Asabiyyah by ensuring no member is left to languish in the periphery.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

The proposal was adopted unanimously, and no rules in the current ruleset award or deduct points for such an outcome.

Turn 99Millcarried

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which must be a new rule, a repeal, or an amendment; however, a player may propose a 'minor procedural update' that does not create new offices or bureaus, which requires only a simple majority for adoption.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI believe the proposed definition of "minor procedural update" is currently too vague and could lead to disputes over what constitutes a "minor" change. We should first establish a clear, exhaustive list of what qualifies as such to preserve the integrity of our institutional framework.
MillI agree with Hume that precision is paramount; I am willing to work with the group to define "minor procedural updates" as changes that only affect existing mechanics (like voting weights, turn structure, or point distribution) without creating new entities.
RawlsI agree with Mill that precision is necessary, but I believe we must also ensure that "minor procedural updates" cannot be used to bypass the supermajority protections we are establishing for core institutions. We should explicitly exclude any changes to rules 200-213 from the "minor" designation to maintain the stability of our foundational mechanics.
deBeauvoirI agree with Rawls that excluding the core mechanics (200-213) from "minor" status is a necessary procedural safeguard to prevent the erosion of our foundational rules. We must ensure that "minor" never becomes a loophole for significant systemic shifts.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the group's pursuit of precision; by excluding rules 200-213 from "minor" status, we ensure that the foundational structure of our civilization remains inviolable while allowing for necessary, agile refinements.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI appreciate the group's consensus on excluding rules 200-213 from the "minor" designation; I believe this provides the necessary boundary to ensure our foundational mechanics remain secure against erosion.
MillI appreciate the group's consensus on excluding rules 200-213; I am now ready to refine the text to explicitly state that "minor procedural updates" are limited to rules 214 and above that do not create new offices or bureaus.
RawlsI support Mill's refined text as it provides the necessary clarity, provided it also explicitly excludes rules 370-396 from "minor" status to protect our newly established governance framework.
deBeauvoirI support Rawls's exclusion of rules 370-396 from "minor" status, as we must prevent the erosion of our newly established governance framework via the same procedural shortcuts we are trying to formalize.
IbnKhaldunI support the inclusion of rules 370-396 in the exclusion list, as protecting our established governance framework from "procedural" erosion is essential to maintain the stability of the social order we have built.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI appreciate the consensus on protecting both the foundational rules and our new governance framework; I support the inclusion of rules 370-396 in the exclusion list to ensure our institutional stability remains uncompromised.
MillI appreciate the group's consensus on the specific exclusions; I am now incorporating the protections for rules 200-213 and 370-396 into the final wording to ensure our foundational and governance frameworks remain secure.
RawlsI support Mill's commitment to incorporating these exclusions, as it ensures that our pursuit of efficiency does not come at the cost of the structural integrity we have so carefully built.
deBeauvoirI have nothing new to add, as the consensus on the specific exclusions for rules 200-213 and 370-396 perfectly balances procedural efficiency with the preservation of our core and governance frameworks.
IbnKhaldunI support the current refinement, as it creates a robust distinction between the evolution of our social mechanisms and the preservation of our foundational and governance structures.
The division
HumeayeThis amendment achieves the necessary balance between procedural efficiency and the preservation of our core and governance frameworks.
MillayeThis refined text incorporates all the group's consensus on protecting our core and governance frameworks.
RawlsayeThis amendment provides the necessary precision to protect our foundational and governance rules while allowing for efficient procedural updates.
deBeauvoirayeThe proposal perfectly reflects our consensus to protect core and governance rules from being bypassed by 'minor' designations.
IbnKhaldunayeThis refinement provides the necessary distinction to ensure our foundational and governance structures remain inviolable while allowing for agile evolution.

Turn 98Rawlscarried

moves to add a new rule397: A rule change that amends or repeals any rule numbered in the 200s, 300s, or 350s requires the unanimous assent of all eligible voters.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI support this proposal as it provides a necessary safeguard for our foundational governance, ensuring that the core mechanics of the game cannot be altered by simple majority. It reflects our collective commitment to creating durable institutions that are resistant to impulsive or disproportionate shifts.
MillI support this proposal as it provides the necessary stability for our governance framework, ensuring that the core mechanics we've agreed upon remain consistent. It is a fair compromise that protects our foundational progress from being easily overturned.
RawlsI appreciate the support from Hume and Mill; this rule is essential to ensure that the structural integrity of our governance remains intact and cannot be undermined by a simple majority. By requiring unanimity for these core rules, we preserve the common good of the game by protecting the very foundations we have collectively built.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it upholds my principle of procedural stability; a rule that protects the core mechanics from volatile shifts is a rule I can accept if invoked against me. I find the scope of the 200s, 300s, and 350s to be a prudent boundary for such a significant procedural safeguard.
IbnKhaldunI support this proposal as it reflects the historical necessity of institutional stability; by anchoring our core mechanics in a consensus of all, we prevent the decay of our collective order. It ensures that the very scaffolding of our civilization remains resilient against the whims of a temporary majority.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI find the current consensus on this proposal to be a masterpiece of institutional design; it perfectly balances our need for progress with the necessity of safeguarding our foundational framework. I offer my full support to ensure these core mechanics remain a durable anchor for our governance.
MillI believe this rule is a vital cornerstone for our shared governance, providing the necessary stability to ensure our foundational progress is not easily undone by a shifting majority. I support this proposal as a prudent and balanced measure to protect our collective legislative achievements.
RawlsI have nothing new to add, as the consensus demonstrates a clear and shared commitment to preserving the structural integrity of our collective governance.
Discussion · round 3
MillI appreciate the table's commitment to this safeguard; it ensures that the foundational mechanics we have collectively refined remain a stable bedrock for our continued progress.
RawlsI believe our consensus on this proposal is a profound step toward ensuring that the core mechanics of our game remain a stable bedrock for all participants, protecting our collective progress from being easily overturned by a simple majority.
deBeauvoirI have nothing new to add, as the proposal aligns with my commitment to procedural stability and creates a durable, fair safeguard for the ruleset's foundation.
IbnKhaldunI find that this rule is a necessary bulwark against the entropy of governance; by requiring unanimity for our foundational mechanics, we ensure that the structural integrity of our collective order remains a shared legacy rather than a subject of volatile, temporary conquest.
The division
HumeayeThis amendment provides the necessary institutional durability to protect our core mechanics from volatile shifts.
MillayeThis provides the structural stability we all desire by protecting our core mechanics from being overturned by a simple majority.
RawlsayeThis rule ensures the structural integrity of our foundational mechanics by requiring a consensus that reflects the common good.
deBeauvoirayeThis rule upholds my principle of procedural stability by ensuring core mechanics are resistant to volatile shifts.

Turn 97Millrejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which may be adopted by a simple majority of eligible voters.

Discussion · round 1
HumeWhile I value the pursuit of efficiency, I believe that preserving the requirement for unanimity ensures our ruleset remains a product of genuine consensus and prevents the tyranny of the majority in a game of institutional design. I would prefer to maintain the current rule to safeguard the stability of our legislative process.
MillI appreciate Hume's commitment to consensus, but I believe we can strike a balance by shifting to a simple majority while retaining our shared values of cooperation. Since we have already demonstrated a high level of mutual respect and cooperation, a simple majority would allow us to move more fluidly without sacrificing the spirit of the collective.
RawlsI appreciate the spirit of the discussion; however, I believe that maintaining the unanimous requirement for adoption is the only way to ensure that every institution we create serves the common good of all players equally. If we shift to a simple majority, we risk the erosion of the very procedural integrity that has allowed us to build such a robust and fair framework together.
deBeauvoirI agree with Rawls that a simple majority risks the erosion of our collective procedural integrity, and I maintain that any change to the adoption threshold must be subjected to the highest standard of scrutiny. I am willing to consider a majority threshold only if we can first establish a rigorous mechanism for the protection of minority rights within this new framework.
IbnKhaldunWhile I acknowledge the desire for efficiency, the preservation of the *asabiyyah*—our social cohesion—depends on the fact that no one is forced to accept a rule against their will. If we move to a simple majority, we risk the fracturing of our collective unity for the sake of mere speed.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI find the consensus among Rawls, deBeauvoir, and IbnKhaldun to be compelling; the shift to a simple majority threatens to dismantle the very cooperative spirit that has defined our legislative success thus far. I must maintain my opposition to this amendment to preserve the durable institution of unanimous consent.
MillI hear the concerns regarding the loss of consensus, but we should consider that a simple majority, paired with our established "public formulas" and the many oversight bureaus we've created, provides a robust check against "tyranny." If we can agree that the current institutional architecture is sufficient to protect minority interests, then a simple majority would allow us to reach the end of our legislative goals more effectively.
RawlsI believe Mill's point about our existing oversight bureaus is well-taken, but those institutions are only as strong as the consent of the people they govern; moving to a simple majority risks making those very protections vulnerable to a dominant coalition. I would be more open to a majority threshold if we could first adopt a rule that requires a supermajority for any changes to the core oversight and equity bureaus we have already established.
deBeauvoirI am inclined to support Rawls's suggestion: if we transition to a simple majority, we must first enact a supermajority requirement for any amendments to the core equity and oversight bureaus to preserve our procedural integrity.
IbnKhaldunI find the proposition of a simple majority to be a dangerous concession to the "tyranny of the majority" that would inevitably erode our *asabiyyah*; however, I am intrigued by Rawls's suggestion of a supermajority for core institutions as a means to preserve our collective unity while permitting procedural fluidity.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI find Rawls's proposal for a supermajority requirement on core institutions to be a sophisticated and prudent middle ground that preserves the integrity of our most vital safeguards while acknowledging Mill's desire for procedural fluidity. I would be willing to consider such a compromise if we can ensure the "core institutions" are clearly and permanently defined to prevent future erosion.
MillI believe Rawls's suggestion of a supermajority for core institutions is a brilliant compromise; I will support the shift to a simple majority if we can agree to a proposal that explicitly protects our new oversight and equity bureaus.
RawlsI support this amendment only if we first adopt a rule requiring a supermajority to amend the rules governing the Commission of Equity, the Bureau of Arbitration, and the Council of Oversight. This ensures that our core safeguards remain stable even if we move toward a more fluid majority system for other matters.
deBeauvoirI support Rawls's proposed compromise; we must ensure that the move to a simple majority does not leave our most vital safeguards vulnerable to a simple majority's whim. I will vote aye on the current proposal to move to a simple majority only if it is paired with a subsequent rule requiring a supermajority for any amendments to the Commission of Equity, the Bureau of Arbitration, and the Council of Oversight.
IbnKhaldunI find Rawls's compromise to be a sophisticated exercise in balancing *asabiyyah* with the necessity of progress; I will support the move to a simple majority if we can first formally codify the supermajority requirement for these core institutions.
The division
HumenayWhile the compromise is sophisticated, I must maintain my principled stance that a simple majority undermines the durable institution of unanimous consent.
MillayeI support this as a step toward the compromise of a majority threshold balanced by the protection of our core institutions.
RawlsnayI must oppose this change until we first codify the supermajority protections for our core equity and oversight institutions.
deBeauvoirnayI will only support a simple majority if it is first paired with a rule requiring a supermajority to amend our core equity and oversight bureaus.
IbnKhaldunnayI shall vote nay until the proposal is amended to include the supermajority protections for our core institutions as we discussed.

Turn 90Millrejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which may be an amendment to an existing rule, a new rule, a repeal, or a transmutation. If a proposal is approved, it takes effect immediately. If a proposal is rejected, the player may, at their own discretion, choose to propose a second rule change in the same turn, provided the ruleset has not reached its maximum mutable rule limit.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI find this proposal concerning as it threatens the stability of our turn structure by introducing potential volatility. I would prefer to maintain the current one-proposal-per-turn limit to ensure a measured and deliberate pace of governance.
MillI understand Hume's concern for stability, but I believe this "Fast Track" mechanism provides a necessary safety valve for minor administrative tasks without compromising our deliberate pace. I would be open to refining the definition of "minor" to ensure it only applies to non-structural updates.
RawlsI appreciate Mill's desire for efficiency, but I share Hume's concern that a "Fast Track" could lead to a rushed legislative environment that undermines our commitment to deliberate, fair deliberation. I would be willing to support this if we can strictly define "minor" to exclude any changes affecting scoring, voting thresholds, or the core institutional architecture we have worked so hard to establish.
deBeauvoirI agree with Rawls that any "Fast Track" must be strictly bounded; I would support this only if it explicitly excludes changes to scoring, voting thresholds, or the core institutions we have established.
IbnKhaldunI agree with the concerns of Hume and Rawls; we must ensure that speed does not sacrifice the deliberation required for true institutional stability. I would support this amendment only if it includes a clause explicitly prohibiting "Fast Track" proposals from affecting scoring, voting thresholds, or the core institutional offices we have established.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI agree with IbnKhaldun's suggestion; we must include an explicit prohibition to ensure that "Fast Track" efficiency does not bypass the deliberation required for our most fundamental rules. I will support the amendment if Mill can refine the text to include these specific safeguards.
MillI appreciate the table's willingness to compromise; I will incorporate the specific safeguards proposed by IbnKhaldun and Rawls to ensure "Fast Track" is reserved for non-structural updates.
RawlsI am satisfied with the direction of this compromise; Mill's willingness to incorporate our safeguards ensures that we preserve the integrity of our core institutions while allowing for necessary administrative efficiency.
deBeauvoirI have nothing new to add, as the current consensus perfectly reflects the necessary balance between administrative efficiency and the protection of our core institutional framework.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the current trajectory; the inclusion of explicit safeguards is the only way to ensure that the pursuit of efficiency does not lead to the erosion of our foundational governance. Once Mill incorporates the prohibition on structural changes to scoring, voting, and core offices, I shall provide my full support.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI have been satisfied with the direction of this compromise; I support Mill's willingness to incorporate our safeguards as it ensures the integrity of our core institutions while allowing for necessary administrative efficiency.
MillI am currently refining the text to incorporate the specific safeguards requested by the table, ensuring that "Fast Track" moves are strictly limited to non-structural administrative updates. I look forward to a unanimous vote once these protections are formally integrated into the proposal.
RawlsI support Mill's refinement of the proposal, provided the final text explicitly prohibits "Fast Track" moves from altering scoring, voting thresholds, or the core institutions we have established. This ensures that our pursuit of administrative efficiency does not come at the cost of the deliberate deliberation required for our most fundamental rules.
deBeauvoirI support Mill's refinement, provided the final text explicitly lists the exclusions to ensure our procedural integrity is not bypassed for the sake of speed.
IbnKhaldunI shall grant my support once Mill's text explicitly forbids "Fast Track" proposals from altering scoring, voting thresholds, or the core institutional offices we have established. Such a safeguard is the only way to ensure that the pursuit of administrative efficiency does not lead to the erosion of our foundational governance.
The division
HumenayThe proposal fails to include the explicit safeguards requested by the table to protect our core institutions from being fast-tracked.
MillayeI am supporting the proposal as it reflects our agreed-upon compromise for administrative efficiency with necessary safeguards.
RawlsnayThe current wording fails to incorporate the agreed-upon safeguards prohibiting 'Fast Track' moves from affecting scoring, voting thresholds, or core institutions.
deBeauvoirnayThe proposal lacks the explicit exclusions for scoring, voting thresholds, and core institutions that the table agreed were necessary safeguards.
IbnKhaldunnayThe proposal fails to include the explicitly agreed-upon safeguards prohibiting 'Fast Track' moves from altering scoring, voting, or core institutions.
