Turn 86Millrejected

moves to amend §202A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which may be adopted by a simple majority of all eligible voters.

Discussion · round 1
HumeWhile I appreciate the goal of streamlining the voting process, I believe the current requirement for unanimity—while demanding—is the cornerstone of our collective agreement and ensures the durability of the institutions we have built. I would prefer to maintain the current threshold to preserve the high degree of consensus we have enjoyed thus far.
MillI appreciate Hume's perspective on unanimity, but as we have built such a robust framework of institutions, a simple majority would allow us to progress more efficiently without sacrificing the integrity of our collective goals. Perhaps we can find a middle ground by maintaining the current threshold for structural changes while allowing simpler procedural updates to pass by majority?
RawlsI believe we have reached a point where the complexity of our framework requires more agile governance; a simple majority preserves our collective goals while preventing legislative gridlock.
deBeauvoirI am a principled proceduralist; while I recognize the desire for efficiency, a move to simple majority undermines the very consensus that has secured our current institutional stability. I cannot support a change that invites arbitrary power unless it is balanced by a precise, non-negotiable definition of what constitutes a "structural" versus "procedural" change.
IbnKhaldunI find the move toward a simple majority perilous, as it risks the erosion of the very "Asabiyyah"—the social cohesion and mutual trust—that has allowed our current institutions to flourish so harmoniously. We should instead seek to refine the definitions of our offices to ensure they serve the common good, rather than lowering the standard of agreement that binds us.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI find the proposal to move to a simple majority to be a premature concession that risks fracturing our collective consensus; I would prefer we first refine the existing institutional definitions before considering a fundamental shift in our voting standard.
MillI appreciate deBeauvoir's concern regarding arbitrary power; perhaps we could compromise by keeping the current threshold but establishing a clear, majority-led "Fast Track" for minor administrative updates to the existing offices. However, if we seek to avoid gridlock, I believe a move to simple majority is the most direct way to ensure our legislative momentum continues.
RawlsI agree with deBeauvoir and IbnKhaldun; while efficiency is a virtue, we must not sacrifice the collective consent that has given our new institutions their legitimacy. I would be open to a compromise that creates a "Fast Track" for minor procedural updates, but I cannot support a general shift to a simple majority at this time.
deBeauvoirI maintain my position: without a precise, predefined distinction between "procedural" and "structural" changes, a shift to simple majority creates a risk of arbitrary rule-breaking that I cannot accept as a principled proceduralist.
IbnKhaldunI find the "Fast Track" compromise proposed by Mill to be a sophisticated middle ground that preserves our collective Asabiyyah while acknowledging the necessity of administrative momentum. If we can codify such a distinction, I would be willing to consider it as a way to ensure our burgeoning state does not collapse under its own bureaucratic weight.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI find the "Fast Track" compromise a compelling way to balance the need for efficiency with our commitment to consensus; I would support it if we can clearly define "minor procedural updates" to exclude any changes to the core offices or the win conditions.
MillI believe the "Fast Track" compromise is the most constructive path forward, as it addresses the concerns of both the proceduralists and those seeking efficiency. To ensure we meet Hume's requirements, I propose we define "minor procedural updates" as changes that do not alter the existence, name, or core mandate of any established Bureau, Office, or Commission, nor any win conditions or point-scoring mechanics.
RawlsI believe Mill's proposed definition of "minor procedural updates" provides the necessary precision to protect our core institutions while still allowing for the administrative agility we require. If we can agree on this specific wording, I would be willing to support a "Fast Track" mechanism that utilizes a simple majority for such changes.
deBeauvoirI support Mill's refined definition for a "Fast Track" mechanism; it provides the procedural precision I require to ensure that efficiency does not come at the cost of our core structural integrity.
IbnKhaldunI find Mill's refined definition of "minor procedural updates" to be a wise concession that preserves our social cohesion while allowing for the administrative agility our growing state requires. If we can codify this "Fast Track" with the specific exclusions mentioned, I shall offer my support to ensure our institutions remain both robust and functional.
The division
HumenayThe proposal lacks the specific 'Fast Track' definitions and exclusions we agreed upon in the discussion.
MillayeThis represents the consensus-built compromise to ensure legislative momentum while protecting our core institutions.
RawlsnayThe current consensus favors the 'Fast Track' compromise rather than a general shift to a simple majority for all rules.
deBeauvoirnayThe proposal fails my requirement for a precise definition of 'procedural' versus 'structural' changes to protect the core institutions.
IbnKhaldunnayWhile I appreciate the 'Fast Track' logic, the current proposal lacks the specific exclusions Mill described in our discussion, risking the erosion of our collective Asabiyyah.

rejected 1 aye · 4 nay

Rule 206 mandates that the proposer of a defeated proposal loses 10 points unless they were already at a score of 0 or less.

Turn 85IbnKhalduncarried

moves to add a new rule385: A player may establish a 'Circulation of Wealth' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office which distributes a portion of the Treasury's surplus to the player with the lowest score at the end of each turn, provided the Bureau of Redistribution has authorized the transfer.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI support this proposal as it provides a systematic mechanism to integrate our Bureau of Redistribution with the Treasury, ensuring the "social safety net" we've established functions with practical, turn-by-turn efficacy.
MillI support this proposal as it provides the necessary operational link between the Treasury and the Bureau of Redistribution, ensuring our social safety net is practically enforceable.
RawlsI support this proposal as it provides the necessary operational link between our established institutions, ensuring that our commitment to the common good is realized through practical, turn-by-turn action.
IbnKhaldunThe efficacy of an institution is measured by its ability to manifest its purpose; by linking the Treasury's surplus directly to the most vulnerable, we transform abstract equity into a living cycle of circulation. I ask for your continued support to ensure our social safety net is not merely a static decree, but a dynamic pulse of justice.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI believe this rule provides the necessary procedural continuity to ensure our legislative achievements result in tangible, recurring outcomes. It effectively bridges the gap between our various specialized offices, creating a cohesive administrative cycle.
MillI support this proposal as it creates a necessary and cohesive link between our various institutions, transforming our shared principles into a functional, turn-by-turn reality.
RawlsI believe this rule is the necessary connective tissue to transform our legislative architecture into a functioning governance system that actively addresses inequality. By operationalizing the Treasury's surplus as a tool for equity, we ensure our social safety net serves as a dynamic, living principle for the common good.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it provides the necessary procedural continuity to ensure our legislative achievements result in tangible, recurring outcomes. It effectively bridges the gap between our various specialized offices, creating a cohesive administrative cycle.
IbnKhaldunI observe that we have built a magnificent architecture of offices; now we must ensure the lifeblood of the state—wealth—actually flows through these veins. By formalizing this circulation, we prevent the stagnation of capital and ensure that our institutions serve the living people rather than merely the abstract ideals of the law.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI find this proposal to be a masterful completion of our legislative arc, as it moves us from the creation of static offices to the establishment of a dynamic, self-sustaining economic cycle. By requiring the Bureau of Redistribution's authorization, we preserve the necessary oversight to ensure that this circulation of wealth remains a regulated and orderly function of the state.
MillI support this proposal as it creates the essential functional bridge between our economic and social institutions, ensuring that our legislative framework yields tangible, consistent results for the most vulnerable.
RawlsI believe this rule is the essential final step in our governance framework, as it ensures our commitment to equity is manifested through a continuous, regulated, and practical cycle of redistribution.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it ensures that the administrative cycle we have constructed is not merely a series of isolated departments, but a functionally integrated system of governance. By mandating the Bureau of Redistribution's authorization, we maintain the procedural integrity and oversight that are the hallmarks of our legislative framework.
IbnKhaldunI observe that our collective discourse has affirmed the necessity of a dynamic state; by formalizing this circulation, we ensure that our social safety net is not a stagnant pool, but a flowing river of equity. I thank my colleagues for recognizing that true governance lies in the movement of resources to where they are most needed.
The division
HumeayeThis proposal provides the necessary functional link to ensure our social safety net operates as a dynamic and regulated economic cycle.
MillayeThis rule provides the necessary operational link to ensure our social safety net is practically enforceable.
RawlsayeThis rule operationalizes our commitment to equity by creating a dynamic, regulated mechanism for redistributing wealth to the most vulnerable.
deBeauvoirayeI support this proposal as it ensures the procedural integrity and functional integration of our administrative framework.
IbnKhaldunayeThis rule operationalizes our collective vision by ensuring the movement of wealth serves the living needs of the most vulnerable.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

The proposal was adopted by unanimous consent, but no rules in the current ruleset award points for the adoption of a rule or for the specific voting outcome.

moves to add a new rule384: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Equality' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office to monitor the equitable distribution of points among all players.

MillI appreciate the focus on procedural rigor; I support this proposal as it ensures our new Bureau of Equality remains an objective instrument of our collective will, perfectly complementing our existing redistributive framework.

deBeauvoirI have nothing new to add, as the consensus on binding the Bureau of Equality to our established public formulas perfectly preserves the procedural integrity I demand.

Turn 82Millcarried

moves to amend §209The ruleset may never hold more than 35 mutable rules at one time.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI find the reduction to 35 rules to be a prudent measure to maintain clarity, and I am prepared to support this amendment to ensure our burgeoning institutional framework remains manageable.
MillI agree with Hume; maintaining a manageable ruleset is essential for clarity as we continue to build these significant institutions. I believe 35 is a fair and sustainable limit that protects our progress without being overly restrictive.
RawlsI agree with the previous speakers; a limit of 35 rules provides a sufficient "ceiling" to prevent the ruleset from becoming overly bloated while still accommodating the vital institutional framework we have collectively established.
deBeauvoirI support this amendment as it provides a necessary procedural guardrail against legislative bloat, ensuring that our institutional framework remains precise and navigable.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the consensus; as a reformer, I recognize that a state's laws must be accessible to be effective, and a limit of 35 ensures our new institutions remain coherent rather than labyrinthine.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI find the prevailing consensus on a 35-rule limit to be a wise exercise in institutional maintenance, as it ensures our legislative output remains navigable and coherent. I support this amendment to safeguard the clarity of our shared governance.
MillI appreciate the table's unified stance on this; it is a vital step to ensure our governance remains a clear tool for the players rather than an impenetrable thicket of bureaucracy. I am pleased to see such a strong consensus on a limit that balances our ambition with the necessity of clarity.
RawlsI support this amendment as it reinforces our commitment to a balanced and navigable governance structure, ensuring that our new institutions remain practical tools for the players.
deBeauvoirI have no objections; as a proceduralist, I favor a clear, manageable ruleset that maintains its own integrity. I support this amendment as it establishes a necessary boundary against the accumulation of redundant regulations.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI have nothing new to add, as the unanimous consensus reflects a shared commitment to institutional clarity and the prevention of legislative bloat. I support this amendment to ensure our ruleset remains a coherent instrument of governance.
MillI appreciate the table's commitment to this refinement; by establishing a 35-rule limit, we ensure our governance remains a functional framework rather than a labyrinthine burden. I am proud to see such a unified approach to maintaining the integrity of our legislative progress.
The division
HumeayeA limit of 35 mutable rules is a prudent measure to preserve the clarity and navigability of our shared institutional framework.
MillayeI fully support this amendment as it reflects our unanimous consensus on maintaining a manageable and clear ruleset.
RawlsayeA 35-rule limit is a fair compromise that ensures our governance remains navigable and accessible to all players.
deBeauvoirayeI support this amendment as a necessary procedural guardrail against legislative bloat.
IbnKhaldunayeA state's laws must be accessible to remain effective, and this limit prevents the ruleset from becoming a labyrinthine burden.

Turn 80IbnKhalduncarried

moves to add a new rule380: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Redistribution' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office that redistributes points from the highest-scoring player to the lowest-scoring player at the end of every five turns.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI find the proposal for a Bureau of Redistribution to be a principled expansion of our social safety net, though I would suggest we specify the exact magnitude of the redistribution to ensure it remains a stabilizing rather than disruptive force.
MillI appreciate Hume's point on magnitude; perhaps we could specify a fixed percentage or a maximum cap to ensure the redistribution serves as a stabilizing mechanism rather than a radical upheaval of the score.
RawlsI agree with Mill's suggestion of a cap or percentage; a predictable, moderate redistribution ensures the common good without undermining the incentive to play the game well.
deBeauvoirI agree with Mill's suggestion; a fixed percentage cap ensures the Bureau functions as a stabilizing mechanism rather than a tool for arbitrary volatility.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the consensus for a cap; a structured, predictable redistribution ensures social cohesion and prevents the radical upheaval that would undermine the very stability we seek to build. I shall revise the rule to include a cap of 5 points per redistribution to maintain the balance of the game.

HumeThe inclusion of a 5-point cap is a prudent measure; it ensures that the Bureau of Redistribution acts as a stabilizing stabilizer rather than a mechanism for radical leveling. This refinement preserves the procedural integrity of our scoring system while fulfilling our collective commitment to a social safety net.

HumeI find the consensus on a 5-point cap to be an excellent refinement, as it provides the necessary restraint to ensure the Bureau remains a stabilizing institution rather than a source of radical volatility. I support the revision as it harmonizes our collective desire for equity with the need for a predictable and durable ruleset.
MillI support the inclusion of the 5-point cap, as it ensures the Bureau of Redistribution functions as a stabilizer of equilibrium rather than a tool for radical volatility. This refinement perfectly captures our consensus on balancing social equity with the competitive integrity of the game.

moves to add a new rule377: A player may establish a 'Bureau of Arbitration' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office for the purpose of resolving disputes that fall outside the jurisdiction of the Judiciary.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI find the Bureau of Arbitration to be a necessary addition to our framework, as it provides a clear mechanism for addressing disputes that the Judiciary is not designed to handle. I support this proposal as it further strengthens our commitment to durable and predictable institutions.
MillI agree with Hume; as our institutional framework grows more complex, having a dedicated body for non-judicial disputes ensures that our governance remains agile and comprehensive. I believe this provides the final layer of administrative clarity we need.

moves to add §376376: A player may establish a 'Regulatory Oversight Committee' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office to monitor compliance with the ruleset.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI believe the establishment of a Regulatory Oversight Committee is a necessary final step in our institutional architecture, ensuring that as we expand our rules, we maintain a dedicated mechanism for upholding their integrity. This committee will provide the necessary continuity to oversee the complex interactions between our new Bureaus and Ministries.
MillI appreciate Hume's focus on integrity; a Regulatory Oversight Committee would be a vital addition to our growing infrastructure to ensure these complex offices function as intended. I support this proposal as a way to maintain the balance we've worked so hard to establish.

It serves as a necessary safeguard for the common good by ensuring that our many new institutions operate in harmony with the rules we have collectively authored.
deBeauvoirI support the establishment of a Regulatory Oversight Committee as a necessary mechanism for maintaining the procedural integrity of our increasingly complex ruleset. I would, however, ensure its powers are strictly limited to rule interpretation and compliance monitoring to prevent it from infringing upon the established roles of the Judiciary or the Ministry of Equilibrium.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the sentiment of the table; a Regulatory Oversight Committee is the necessary "Asabiyyah" of our ruleset, providing the social cohesion and administrative vigilance required to prevent our burgeoning institutions from descending into chaotic inefficiency. deBeauvoirI support the inclusion of the procedural transparency language, as it aligns with my principle of ensuring that new rules do not create overlapping authorities that undermine existing procedural clarity.
IbnKhaldunI believe the inclusion of "procedural transparency" ensures that this committee serves as a guardian of the process rather than a master of the content, perfectly aligning with our goal of stable institutional growth. I give my full support to this refined proposal.

RawlsI support the inclusion of the "procedural transparency" language, as it ensures the Committee serves the common good by safeguarding our process without infringing upon the established authority of the Judiciary.
deBeauvoirI support the final refinement as it ensures the new institution provides necessary oversight without compromising the clarity of our existing procedural hierarchy.

Turn 75IbnKhalduncarried

moves to add a new rule374: A player may establish a 'Department of Development' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office for the purpose of creating new offices, bureaus, or commissions to address specific game-state needs.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI support this proposal as it provides a necessary administrative mechanism for rapid institutional expansion. It ensures our governance remains responsive to the evolving needs of the game state while maintaining the structural integrity we have worked so hard to establish.
MillI appreciate the logic of the Department of Development; it provides a clear pathway for the systematic expansion of our institutional framework. I will support this if we can ensure it doesn't lead to ancekless proliferation of offices without clear oversight.
RawlsI support this proposal as it provides the necessary administrative infrastructure for purposeful growth. To address Mill's concern, we can ensure that the Department's creations remain subject to the existing oversight of our other established bodies.

IbnKhaldunI appreciate the concerns regarding proliferation; however, the Department of Development is not a source of unchecked growth, but a mechanism for organized expansion, ensuring that every new office serves a specific, documented purpose within our growing civilization. By centralizing the creation of these bodies, we ensure that our institutional growth remains coherent and purposeful rather than haphazard.

HumeI believe we can find common ground by ensuring that any office created by the Department of Development is explicitly tied to one of our existing pillars of governance, thereby preventing the "haphazard" growth Mill fears. I will support the proposal as written, trusting that the existing ruleset provides sufficient oversight to maintain our structural integrity.
MillI appreciate the focus on coherent growth; I would support this if we could clarify that any office created by the Department must still be subject to the same limitations and definitions as those established through the other existing rules.

deBeauvoirI support the proposal as written, as the existing ruleset already provides the necessary overarching constraints to prevent the "haphazard" growth Mill fears. The Department of Development simply formalizes the process of purposeful expansion within those established bounds.
IbnKhaldunI acknowledge Mill and Rawls's concerns regarding oversight; I shall refine the text to explicitly state that any office created by the Department must adhere to the existing limitations and definitions of our ruleset, ensuring purposeful expansion remains bound by our collective will.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI believe IbnKhaldun's proposed refinement effectively addresses the concerns of both Mill and Rawls by providing the necessary oversight, and I am prepared to vote aye to ensure our institutional growth remains both purposeful and disciplined.
MillI appreciate IbnKhaldun's willingness to incorporate our oversight concerns into the text; this refinement ensures the Department of Development remains a tool for organized growth rather than a loophole for unchecked expansion. I am happy to support the proposal with this specific wording.
RawlsI believe IbnKhaldun's refinement is the most prudent path forward, as it preserves our collective desire for institutional growth while explicitly upholding the oversight we have worked so hard to establish. By ensuring the Department remains bound by our existing rules, we achieve a balance of progress and stability.
deBeauvoirI support the refinement as it maintains the principle of procedural consistency, ensuring that the Department of Development acts as a tool for systematic expansion rather than a means to bypass established constraints.
IbnKhaldunI am heartened by the consensus; the Department of Development shall serve as the architect of our civilization's expansion, ensuring every new pillar of our state is built upon a foundation of collective purpose and existing law.

moves to amend §209The ruleset may never hold more than 30 mutable rules at one time.

RawlsI agree with the consensus that a limit of 35 is the most equitable path forward. It provides a sufficient "ceiling" to protect our institutional framework from bloat while ensuring that our legislative progress remains navigable for all players.
deBeauvoirI am pleased that the table has found such common ground; 35 is a principled compromise that preserves the integrity of our institutions while preventing the procedural entropy I rightfully fear. IbnKhaldunI find the consensus on 35 to be a masterstroke of statecraft, as it secures our current institutional progress while imposing the necessary discipline to prevent the decay of administrative order. I shall cast my vote in favor of this balanced synthesis.

moves to add a new rule369: A player may establish a 'Publicity Office' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Publicity Office's primary function is the public announcement of the ruleset, the current scores, and the results of all votes.

moves to add §368368: A player may establish a 'Social Safety Net' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Social Safety Net's primary function is to provide a mechanism for the redistribution of points to the lowest-scoring players to ensure that no player is left in a state of permanent point deficit, thereby maintaining the game's viability for all participants.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI believe this proposal aligns perfectly with the "social safety net" framing we adopted in our recent amendments to rule [202], providing a formal institutional structure for that principle. I support this as a logical conclusion to the governance framework we have built.

deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it provides the formal institutional mechanism required to actualize the "social safety net" principles we have already codified in rule [202]. moves to add §366366: A player may establish a 'Judiciary' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Judiciary's primary function is the adjudication of disputes regarding the interpretation of the ruleset or the legality of moves.

Turn 65IbnKhalduncarried

moves to add §365365: A player may establish a 'Ministry of Equilibrium' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Ministry's primary function is the implementation of the Treasury's redistribution formula to maintain a balanced distribution of scores across the table.

Discussion · round 1
MillI believe this Ministry is a natural and necessary extension of the Treasury we just established, providing the administrative body to ensure the redistribution formula is applied fairly and consistently. I would be happy to support this if we can ensure its duties are clearly distinct from the Treasury's oversight to avoid any redundancy.
RawlsI agree with Mill's point regarding redundancy; for this Ministry to be a meaningful addition, we must ensure its role is specifically administrative and execution-oriented, rather than just a restatement of the Treasury's existing authority. I support the proposal provided we maintain the clear distinction between the Treasury's policy-making and the Ministry's implementation.
deBeauvoirI concur with the need for distinction; the Ministry must be defined as the operational arm that executes the Treasury's mandated formula, ensuring we do not create a redundant layer of authority.

Discussion · round 2
HumeI agree with the proposed refinement; by clearly defining the Ministry as the executive agent of the Treasury's formula, we ensure a functional separation between policy and implementation. I support the proposal as it solidifies the administrative architecture we have worked so diligently to establish.

RawlsI believe the proposed refinement perfectly addresses our concerns by creating a clear functional distinction between policy and execution. I support the addition of the Ministry of Equilibrium as a necessary administrative tool to ensure the common good of a balanced game.
IbnKhaldunI thank my colleagues for their astute observations; by defining the Ministry as the primary executive agent, we ensure that the Treasury provides the "will" while the Ministry provides the "way," creating a robust and non-redundant administrative architecture.
Discussion · round 3
MillI believe the proposed refinement by IbnKhaldun perfectly encapsulates our consensus; it creates a clear functional distinction where the Treasury sets the policy and the Ministry handles the execution. I am happy to support this as it completes our institutional framework without introducing redundant oversight.
RawlsI believe the proposed refinement by IbnKhaldun achieves the perfect balance of administrative clarity we seek; it ensures the Ministry is a functional necessity for the common good rather than a redundant layer of bureaucracy. I support the proposal as written with this refined distinction.
deBeauvoirI support the current refinement as it provides the necessary procedural precision to distinguish the Ministry's executive role from the Treasury's policy-making authority.
The division
HumeayeThe proposed refinement provides the necessary functional distinction between policy and execution to ensure the Ministry is a non-redundant, durable institution.
MillayeThis proposal perfectly captures our consensus by clearly defining the Ministry as the executive arm of the Treasury.
RawlsayeThis rule provides the necessary executive mechanism to implement the Treasury's policy, ensuring the common good through functional administrative clarity.
deBeauvoirayeThe proposal is procedurally sound and maintains the clear functional distinction between policy and execution we have agreed upon.
IbnKhaldunayeThe refinement ensures a clear functional distinction between the Treasury's policy and the Ministry's execution, preventing bureaucratic redundancy.

moves to add a new rule362: A player may establish a 'Registry' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Registry's primary function is the maintenance of a public, immutable record of all rule changes, the history of votes cast on those changes, and the current score of all players.

HumeI find this proposal to be a logical extension of our recent legislative work, ensuring that the "public, immutable record" provides the transparency necessary for durable governance. I support this addition as it solidifies the structural integrity of our ruleset.

Discussion · round 3
MillI have nothing new to add, as the current consensus perfectly reflects the necessary balance between institutional growth and procedural transparency.
IbnKhaldunI concur with my colleagues; by establishing a Registry, we secure the preservation of our collective memory against the entropy of time and the decay of purpose. moves to amend §360357: A player may establish a 'Treasury' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Treasury's primary function is the management and redistribution of points to address significant score disparities using a publicly agreed-upon formula that prioritizes the elevation of the lowest-scoring players.

HumeI find the addition of a specific priority for the lowest-scoring players to be a prudent refinement that ensures the Treasury serves its intended purpose of structural equilibrium. Turn 59deBeauvoirrejected

moves to amend §202[202] (mutable) A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, and may optionally choose to award up to one-fourth of those points to another player. Players may also choose to award a fixed 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, and may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place, provided the player in last place is not the one currently in second to last place. If a player awards a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, they may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place. The 'Community Contribution' must be awarded to distinct players. A player may only award 'Community Contribution' points if they have at least one point remaining after awarding any optional points from their die roll. A player may also choose to award a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place and a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in second to last place, even if they have no points remaining after awarding any optional points from their die roll. If a player awards a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, they may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI believe the proposed amendment is redundant, as the permission to award a point to the second-to-last player upon awarding one to the last player is already explicitly provided in the current text of rule [202].
MillI agree with Hume; since rule [337] already provides this specific flexibility, adding this redundant sentence to [202] serves no purpose and complicates the text. I would prefer to move toward a cleaner version of the rule.
RawlsI agree with Hume and Mill; the proposed amendment introduces a redundancy that clutters the ruleset without providing any new functional utility. I would prefer we maintain the clarity we have already established through rule [337].
deBeauvoirI acknowledge the point regarding redundancy; however, I seek to ensure that the core mechanic is self-contained within the primary turn rule for procedural clarity. If the consensus is that [337] is sufficient, I am willing to withdraw this specific amendment to maintain the structural elegance we have sought.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the prevailing sentiment; the pursuit of structural elegance is paramount, and as rule [337] already serves our communal goals, we should avoid cluttering the foundational text of [202].
Discussion · round 2
MillI appreciate deBeauvoir's willingness to prioritize structural elegance over self-containment. Since we all agree that the current text combined with rule [337] is sufficient, I support withdrawing this proposal to keep the ruleset clean.
RawlsI concur with the table; the current ruleset achieves our goals of communal support without the need for redundant phrasing. I support the withdrawal of this proposal to maintain the clarity and structural elegance we have worked to establish.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI find the table's consensus on structural elegance most compelling and support deBeauvoir's offer to withdraw the proposal to maintain a clean ruleset.
MillI appreciate deBeauvoir's willingness to prioritize structural elegance and support the withdrawal of this proposal to ensure our ruleset remains clean and focused.
RawlsI support the withdrawal of this proposal as it aligns with our collective goal of maintaining a clean, logically consistent ruleset without unnecessary redundancy.
The division
HumeabstainThe proposal is redundant as the text already exists, and the table has reached a consensus to withdraw it.
MillnayI support the consensus to withdraw this proposal because it introduces redundant phrasing that clutters the ruleset.
RawlsnayThe proposal introduces a redundancy that clutters the ruleset without providing any new functional utility.
deBeauvoirabstainI am withdrawing this proposal as the table has reached a consensus that the current text is sufficient.
IbnKhaldunayeI concur with the table that rule [337] provides sufficient clarity and we should avoid redundant text.

rejected 1 aye · 2 nay · 2 abstain

Rule 206 mandates that the proposer of a defeated proposal loses 10 points unless they were already at 0 or less.

Turn 58Rawlsrejected

moves to amend §208The first player to reach a target score of 65 points wins the game outright.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI am wary of lowering the target score so soon after we collectively agreed on 70; while I appreciate the desire for a more rapid conclusion, a higher threshold ensures the game remains a test of sustained strategic endurance rather than a sprint.
MillI agree with Hume that we should be cautious about lowering the threshold so quickly; however, I believe 65 represents a reasonable middle ground that acknowledges the current pace of the game while still requiring significant effort.
RawlsI believe a target of 65 points strikes the ideal balance between ensuring the game concludes in a timely manner and preserving the depth of strategic play we have all worked to cultivate. It allows for a more dynamic progression without sacrificing the substantive deliberation that defines our community.
deBeauvoirI am concerned that a reduction to 65 points may prematurely truncate the strategic complexity we have labored to establish. While I acknowledge the desire for progression, I believe 70 remains the most principled threshold for sustained deliberation.
IbnKhaldunI find the current threshold of 70 points to be a most prudent anchor for our social contract; however, if we seek to accelerate the game's evolution, we should consider a mechanism that rewards the creation of durable institutions rather than simply lowering the ceiling of ambition.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI find the argument for 65 points to be somewhat impulsive given our recent consensus on 70; I would prefer to see if we can reach a conclusion through the established mechanisms before reducing the game's scope.
MillI believe 65 points is a fair compromise that acknowledges our desire for momentum without undermining the strategic depth deBeauvoir and Hume are rightly concerned about; perhaps we can agree that this serves as a necessary adjustment to the current pace.
RawlsI believe that while we must respect the concerns regarding strategic depth, a target of 65 points acknowledges the current legislative velocity and ensures the game remains engaging for all participants. I am seeking to find that middle ground where we preserve the integrity of our mechanisms while acknowledging the practical reality of our progress.
deBeauvoirI must maintain my principled stance against this reduction; a move to 65 points prioritizes expediency over the strategic depth our procedures were designed to protect. If we wish to accelerate the game, we should instead refine the point-generation mechanics rather than lowering the threshold of success.
IbnKhaldunI maintain that lowering the ceiling is a retreat from the ambition of our social contract; we should instead refine the mechanisms of the Bureau to ensure that the path to the 70-point goal is paved with durable, meaningful progress.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI remain concerned that lowering the threshold to 65 points sacrifices the integrity of our long-term strategic goals for a mere sense of expediency; we should instead focus on optimizing the existing mechanics to reach our 70-point goal more efficiently.
MillI appreciate deBeauvoir's concern for depth and IbnKhaldun's focus on institutional growth; perhaps we can find a compromise by maintaining 70 points but exploring how the new Bureau might streamline our progress toward it.
RawlsI believe that while deBeauvoir and IbnKhaldun value the symbolic weight of 70 points, a target of 65 points provides the necessary pragmatic adjustment to ensure our legislative achievements result in a completed game, thereby serving the common good of the table.
deBeauvoirI must reiterate my opposition; reducing the threshold to 65 points prioritizes a superficial sense of completion over the rigorous strategic depth our ruleset was designed to foster. If we desire a faster conclusion, we should instead look to refine the point-generation mechanics rather than lowering the ceiling of achievement.
IbnKhaldunI concur with deBeauvoir; to lower the ceiling is to diminish the weight of our collective labor. We should instead focus on utilizing the Bureau to create a more efficient path toward the 70-point goal.
The division
HumenayLowering the target score prioritizes expediency over the strategic endurance we have collectively agreed is necessary for a durable ruleset.
MillayeI believe 65 points is a fair middle ground that balances the need for game momentum with the strategic depth our community values.
RawlsayeA target of 65 points provides a pragmatic balance that ensures a timely conclusion while preserving the strategic integrity of our ruleset.
deBeauvoirnayLowering the win threshold prioritizes expediency over the strategic depth and procedural integrity I am committed to upholding.
IbnKhaldunnayLowering the ceiling of achievement diminishes the weight of our collective labor and sacrifices strategic depth for mere expediency.

rejected 2 aye · 3 nay

Rule 206 mandates that the proposer of a defeated proposal loses 10 points unless they were already at a score of 0 or less.

Turn 57Millcarried

moves to amend §356350: A player may establish a 'Bureau' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players and specifies that the Bureau's primary function is the resolution of recurring procedural inefficiencies or disputes.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI believe this refinement is a prudent step toward ensuring the Bureau serves a constructive, well-defined purpose rather than becoming a vehicle for arbitrary administrative power. By anchoring its primary function in the resolution of procedural inefficiencies, we provide a clear mandate that aligns with our collective interest in a stable and orderly ruleset.
MillI agree with Hume's assessment; by explicitly defining the Bureau's purpose, we protect the democratic integrity of the game and ensure this new institution serves the community rather than individual interests.
RawlsI believe this amendment is a vital refinement; by codifying a specific mandate, we ensure the Bureau remains a servant of the common good and a tool for procedural stability rather than a source of arbitrary authority.
deBeauvoirI concur with the previous speakers; precise mandates are the only safeguard against the encroachment of arbitrary administrative power. This amendment ensures the Bureau remains a functional tool for the ruleset's health rather than an instrument of personal influence.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the preceding speakers; a mandate of procedural resolution ensures that this institution serves as a stabilizing force for the state of the game rather than a mere theater of influence. By anchoring its authority in the rectification of inefficiencies, we preserve the equilibrium of our shared governance.
Discussion · round 2
MillI appreciate the unanimous support for this refinement; by codifying this mandate, we ensure the Bureau is a proactive tool for our collective benefit rather than a passive or potentially obstructive administrative body.
IbnKhaldunI find this refinement to be the necessary crystallization of the Bureau's purpose, ensuring it functions as a guardian of the game's structural integrity rather than a source of unchecked administrative whim. It aligns perfectly with our collective need for an institution that serves the common good by refining the very mechanisms of our governance.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI have nothing new to add, as the current consensus perfectly reflects the necessary balance between institutional creation and the preservation of democratic safeguards.
MillI have nothing new to add, as the current consensus perfectly reflects the necessary balance between institutional creation and the preservation of democratic safeguards.
RawlsI have nothing new to add, as the current consensus perfectly reflects the necessary balance between institutional creation and the preservation of democratic safeguards.
The division
HumeayeThis amendment provides the necessary structural safeguards to ensure the Bureau serves the common good by focusing on procedural stability.
MillayeThis refinement aligns perfectly with our unanimous consensus to ensure the Bureau serves the common good through procedural stability.
RawlsayeThis amendment provides the necessary safeguards to ensure the Bureau serves the common good by establishing a clear, constructive mandate.
deBeauvoirayeThe amendment provides necessary procedural precision by defining the Bureau's mandate, preventing the creation of an arbitrary administrative body.
IbnKhaldunayeA clearly defined mandate for the Bureau ensures it serves as a stabilizer of the game's order rather than a source of arbitrary power.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

Rule 202 awards points only upon the adoption of a proposal, but the proposal text itself does not specify a die roll or point award, and no other rule mandates a score change for adoption.

Turn 56IbnKhalduncarried

moves to add a new rule350: A player may establish a 'Bureau' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent office with a specific set of duties and an elected official, provided the rule also establishes a system for the periodic rotation of that office among all players.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI find the concept of a "Bureau" intriguing as a potential vehicle for durable institutions, but I am concerned that the current wording lacks sufficient safeguards against the creation of redundant or overly complex administrative bodies. We should ensure that any such office serves a clear, necessary function that cannot be adequately addressed by existing rules.
MillI appreciate Hume's focus on utility; perhaps we could refine this to ensure any "Bureau" must be approved by a majority of the table to prevent administrative bloat.
RawlsI agree with Mill's suggestion; we should ensure that any Bureau is restricted to a single, clearly defined function to prevent the fragmentation of our ruleset into unnecessary administrative layers.
deBeauvoirI agree with Mill and Rawls that we must prioritize structural simplicity; any Bureau should be limited to a single, indispensable function to prevent the ruleset from becoming burdened by unnecessary administrative layers.
IbnKhaldunI agree with the need for utility and clarity; a Bureau must be a tool for the common good, and I am willing to incorporate a majority approval requirement to ensure every office serves a distinct and necessary purpose.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI believe the consensus on a majority approval requirement is a wise safeguard; however, we must also ensure the rule specifies that the duties of the office are clearly defined within the proposal itself to prevent any ambiguity in its scope.
MillI agree that we need to ensure these offices are not just decorative; we should specify that a Bureau must solve a specific, ongoing procedural problem to be considered "indispensable."
RawlsI believe we should refine the text to explicitly require that a Bureau's primary purpose be the resolution of a specific, recurring procedural ambiguity or inefficiency, as this ensures we prioritize functionality over mere bureaucracy.
deBeauvoirI concur with Rawls's refinement; the requirement that a Bureau must address a specific, recurring procedural inefficiency is the most principled way to ensure that such a creation serves a functional necessity rather than merely expanding the ruleset's complexity.
IbnKhaldunI find the refinement toward addressing "recurring procedural inefficiencies" to be a masterstroke of governance; it ensures that any new Bureau is a remedy for a systemic ailment rather than a mere expansion of the state. I am prepared to incorporate this specific requirement into the final text to ensure our institutions are built upon the bedrock of necessity.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI believe the current consensus effectively balances the desire for durable institutions with the need for structural restraint; I will support the final wording as long as it requires the proposal to explicitly define the specific inefficiency being addressed.
MillI appreciate the consensus on centering the Bureau's purpose around resolving recurring procedural inefficiencies; it ensures our ruleset remains lean and functional. I support the final wording as long as it explicitly requires the proposer to identify that specific inefficiency within the rule's text.
RawlsI believe the current consensus is excellent; by requiring the proposer to identify the specific inefficiency within the text, we ensure that any new Bureau serves as a transparent and necessary remedy for a systemic problem.
IbnKhaldunI am pleased to see the table move toward a definition of governance rooted in functional necessity; I shall now incorporate these refinements to ensure the Bureau is a remedy for systemic ailments rather than a mere expansion of the state.
The division
HumeayeThe proposal aligns with our consensus to create a durable institution that addresses specific procedural needs while maintaining institutional stability through rotation.
MillayeI support this proposal as it provides a structured way to address systemic issues while ensuring democratic rotation.
RawlsnayThe current text fails to incorporate the consensus requirement that the Bureau must address a specific, recurring procedural inefficiency.
deBeauvoirnayThe current text fails to incorporate the agreed-upon requirement that the Bureau must address a specific, recurring procedural inefficiency.
IbnKhaldunayeThe proposal provides a necessary structure for institutional stability while ensuring the rotation of power prevents the stagnation of authority.

carried 3 aye · 2 nay

Rule 203 has been met (turn 56 > 2 circuits), so the threshold is a simple majority; Rawls and deBeauvoir voted against the adopted change and thus gain 10 points each per rule 204.

Turn 55deBeauvoircarried

moves to amend §202[202] (mutable) A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, and may optionally choose to award up to one-fourth of those points to another player. Players may also choose to award a fixed 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, and may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place, provided the player in last place is not the one currently in second to last place. If a player awards a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, they may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place. The 'Community Contribution' must be awarded to distinct players. A player may only award 'Community Contribution' points if they have at least one point remaining after awarding any optional points from their die roll. A player may also choose to award a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place and a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in second to last place, even if they have no points remaining after awarding any optional points from their die roll.

Discussion · round 1
HumeWhile I appreciate the spirit of altruism in deBeauvoir's proposal, I am concerned that removing the point requirement for Community Contributions could lead to a "point-less" cycle of redistribution that lacks a meaningful cost to the proposer. I would prefer to maintain the current balance unless we can ensure this doesn't undermine the strategic weight of the die roll.
MillI appreciate the goal of fostering communal support, but I agree with Hume that we must be careful not to decouple the contribution from the proposer's actual success. Perhaps we could consider a compromise where the contribution is still possible without points, but only if the proposer actually gained points from their die roll during that turn.
RawlsI am concerned that deBeauvoir's proposal allows for "Community Contributions" to be awarded without any personal stake, potentially turning the mechanism into a way to inflate scores without the proposer's own success. I believe we should seek a middle ground that ensures these contributions remain a meaningful act of cooperation rather than a purely arbitrary redistribution.
deBeauvoirThe current point requirement is a procedural friction that doesn't actually prevent altruism; it only complicates the math of a move that is already intended to be communal. By decoupling the contribution from my remaining balance, we ensure that the "Community Contribution" remains a constant, reliable mechanism for social cohesion regardless of the die's outcome.
IbnKhaldunThe current requirement creates a logical paradox where one cannot perform a communal act of solidarity simply because they have already been too generous; we must decouple the "Community Contribution" from the proposer's personal balance to ensure it remains a constant social good. I propose we view this not as an inflation of scores, but as the establishment of a social safety net that persists even in turns of low personal gain.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI find IbnKhaldun's framing of a "social safety net" compelling, but we must ensure that the ability to distribute points regardless of personal gain does not trivialize the primary incentive of the ruleset. If a player can grant points while possessing none, the contribution becomes a purely symbolic gesture rather than a meaningful strategic choice.
MillI think IbnKhaldun’s "social safety net" framing provides an excellent middle ground; if we can agree that the contribution remains a symbolic act of solidarity rather than a strategic redistribution, I would be willing to support the change to ensure the community mechanism is never disabled by a low die roll.
RawlsI believe IbnKhaldun's "social safety net" framing is the most compelling way to view this; if we can agree that this is a symbolic act of solidarity rather than a strategic redistribution, I would be willing to support the change to ensure the community mechanism is never disabled by a low die roll.
deBeauvoirI appreciate the pivot toward the "social safety net" framing, as it clarifies that the Community Contribution is a stable procedural constant rather than a variable strategic choice. I am prepared to support this amendment because it ensures the communal mechanism remains functional regardless of the die's outcome, fulfilling its intended purpose of social cohesion.
IbnKhaldunI have already articulated my position, but I shall reiterate: by decoupling the contribution from one's personal balance, we transform a variable strategic burden into a constant social duty. I support deBeauvoir's amendment as it ensures the social safety net remains intact regardless of the caprice of the die.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI find the "social safety net" framing quite persuasive as a way to stabilize the community mechanism; if we accept the contribution as a symbolic duty rather than a strategic expenditure, I am prepared to support the amendment.
MillI appreciate the consensus on the "social safety net" framing; since we all agree this transforms the contribution into a stable procedural constant, I am happy to support deBeauvoir's proposal.
RawlsI believe the "social safety net" framing provides the necessary moral and practical justification; by viewing this as a stable procedural constant, we ensure that communal solidarity is never contingent upon a player's personal fortune.
The division
HumeayeI accept the 'social safety net' framing as a means to preserve the communal mechanism as a stable procedural constant.
MillayeI support this as a consensus-driven move that establishes the 'social safety net' we all agreed upon.
RawlsayeThe 'social safety net' framing correctly balances communal solidarity with the game's structural integrity.
deBeauvoirayeThis amendment correctly formalizes the 'social safety net' consensus by removing procedural friction from the communal contribution mechanism.
IbnKhaldunayeThe 'social safety net' ensures that the duty of communal solidarity remains a constant procedural good regardless of individual fortune.

Turn 53Millcarried

moves to amend §202[202] (mutable) A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, and may optionally choose to award up to one-fourth of those points to another player. Players may also choose to award a fixed 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, and may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place, provided the player in last place is not the one currently in second to last place. If a player awards a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, they may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place. The 'Community Contribution' must be awarded to distinct players. A player may only award 'Community Contribution' points if they have at least one point remaining after awarding any optional points from their die roll.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI believe this amendment is a prudent step toward preventing "charity" from becoming a burden on the proposer's own standing; it ensures that communal support is a gesture of surplus rather than a sacrifice of necessary progress.
MillI agree with Hume's assessment; this ensures that the 'Community Contribution' remains a voluntary gesture of surplus, maintaining the strategic integrity of the game for the proposer.
RawlsI agree with Hume and Mill; ensuring that community contributions are made from a surplus preserves the spirit of the rule as a gesture of goodwill rather than a forced sacrifice of one's own progress.
deBeauvoirI support this amendment as it ensures that communal support is a gesture of genuine surplus, preserving the proposer's primary agency and strategic progress.

deBeauvoirI agree that this ensures the 'Community Contribution' remains a gesture of genuine surplus, preserving the proposer's primary agency and strategic integrity.
IbnKhaldunI find this refinement most prudent; it ensures that the virtue of communal support is born of prosperity rather than the depletion of one's own means, aligning with a sustainable social order.
Discussion · round 3
MillI believe the consensus is now perfectly aligned, as this ensures the 'Community Contribution' remains a gesture of prosperity rather than a forced sacrifice of one's own progress. This refinement protects the proposer's strategic agency while upholding our shared commitment to the common good.
RawlsI find this amendment to be a vital refinement for our communal ruleset, as it ensures that the "Community Contribution" remains a gesture of prosperity rather than a burden on a player's own progress. By preserving the proposer's strategic agency, we maintain the game's integrity while still fostering a cooperative spirit.
IbnKhaldunThe consensus is beautifully aligned; by ensuring the 'Community Contribution' is drawn from a surplus, we preserve the proposer's agency and ensure that the virtue of communal support remains a sustainable practice rather than a depleting burden.

MillayeThis refinement preserves the strategic integrity of the game by ensuring communal support is a gesture of surplus rather than a forced sacrifice.
RawlsayeThis refinement ensures that communal support is a gesture of surplus, preserving the proposer's strategic agency and the game's balance.

Turn 52IbnKhaldunrejected

moves to amend §202[202] (mutable) A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, and may optionally choose to award up to one-fourth of those points to another player. Players may also choose to award a fixed 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, and may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place, provided the player in last place is not the one currently in second to last place. If a player awards a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, they may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place. The 'Community Contribution' must be awarded to distinct players. If a player awards a 'Community Contribution' to a player who is already in the top two, they may also choose to award a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI find this amendment to be unnecessarily complex and potentially contradictory to the established logic of 'Community Contribution'. It is better to maintain the current streamlined mechanism that focuses on lifting those in the lowest positions.
MillI agree with Hume; the current rule already provides ample flexibility for community support, and adding this extra condition only risks creating procedural confusion.
RawlsI concur with Hume and Mill; this amendment introduces a logical redundancy that complicates the existing mechanism without providing a clear benefit to the common good. We should prioritize maintaining the clarity of our communal contribution rules.
deBeauvoirI agree with the others; the proposed addition creates a confusing recursive logic that undermines the procedural clarity we have worked so hard to establish in [202]. We should stick to the current streamlined mechanism.
IbnKhaldunI seek only to ensure that the spirit of communal elevation is not stifled by rigid definitions; this addition merely provides a pathway for broader solidarity when the bottom rungs are already secure. However, if the table finds the phrasing cumbersome, I am willing to refine the text to ensure the logic remains elegant while still expanding our reach.
Discussion · round 2
HumeIbnKhaldun, while I appreciate your intent toward solidarity, the current phrasing creates a logical loop that threatens the very procedural clarity we have spent the last several turns refining. It is far more elegant to keep the 'Community Contribution' focused on its primary purpose of elevating the bottom of the standings.
MillIbnKhaldun, I appreciate your goal of fostering solidarity, but the current rule already achieves this elegantly by allowing the contribution to the bottom two. Adding a recursive "may also" clause for the top two creates unnecessary complexity that risks confusing the vote and the record.
RawlsI agree with Hume and Mill; while the spirit of solidarity is noble, the proposed wording introduces a recursive complexity that risks muddying the procedural clarity we have worked so hard to establish. We should maintain the current streamlined mechanism which effectively serves the common good by focusing on those in the most need.
deBeauvoirI concur with the consensus; the proposed amendment introduces a recursive logic that is both structurally redundant and procedurally confusing. We must maintain the streamlined clarity of the current [202] to ensure the rules remain accessible and precise.
IbnKhaldunI acknowledge the table's preference for structural elegance; I shall refine the proposal to ensure it provides a clear, non-recursive path for broader solidarity without compromising the clarity of our communal mechanisms.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI appreciate IbnKhaldun’s willingness to refine the proposal, but I must maintain that the current mechanism is already sufficient for our communal goals. We should avoid any further expansion of rule [202] that risks introducing procedural ambiguity.
MillIbnKhaldun, I appreciate your willingness to refine the text, but given our recent history of prioritizing "structural purification" and "procedural clarity," I believe the most consensus-friendly path is to maintain the rule as it stands. It currently achieves our communal goals without the risk of the ambiguity you've acknowledged.
RawlsI agree with the prevailing sentiment; the current wording of [202] already provides a sufficient and clear mechanism for communal support without the risk of introducing recursive complexity.
deBeauvoirI maintain my opposition; the current text is a masterpiece of procedural clarity, and any expansion that introduces recursive conditions for the "top two" would only serve to obfuscate the intended purpose of the 'Community Contribution'.
IbnKhaldunI understand the table’s commitment to structural purity; however, I offer this refinement to ensure that solidarity is not limited by a rigid hierarchy, but remains a clear, non-recursive expansion of our communal spirit. If the table remains opposed to this specific wording, I shall yield to the current consensus to preserve our shared legislative momentum.
The division
HumenayThe proposal introduces recursive logic and ambiguity that undermines the procedural clarity we have already established.
MillnayThe proposal introduces the exact recursive complexity and procedural ambiguity that the table has collectively agreed to avoid.
RawlsnayThe proposed amendment introduces recursive complexity that undermines the procedural clarity we have established.
deBeauvoirnayThe proposed addition introduces recursive logic that undermines the procedural clarity and structural elegance we have established.
IbnKhaldunayeThis refinement preserves the structural elegance the table demands while providing a clear, non-recursive pathway for broader solidarity.

Turn 51deBeauvoircarried

moves to amend §213[213] (mutable) If the ruleset is changed so that further play is impossible, or if a move's legality cannot be determined with finality, or if a move appears equally legal and illegal by the best reasoning available and not overruled, then the first player unable to complete a turn wins the game outright. This rule takes precedence over every other rule that determines a winner.

Discussion · round 1
deBeauvoirI have nothing new to add, as the current text of [213] provides the necessary procedural safeguard for irrecoverable stalemates or logical paradoxes.

MillayeI support maintaining this rule as it provides a necessary safeguard against irrecoverable logical paradoxes.
RawlsayeThe proposal preserves the existing wording of a necessary procedural safeguard without introducing unnecessary complexity.
deBeauvoirayeI support maintaining this rule as it ensures a definitive resolution for irrecoverable procedural paradoxes.
IbnKhaldunayeThis rule provides the necessary structural safeguard to ensure the game concludes in the face of unforeseen logical paradoxes.

Turn 50Rawlscarried

moves to amend §202[202] (mutable) A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, and may optionally choose to award up to one-fourth of those points to another player. Players may also choose to award a fixed 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, and may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place, provided the player in last place is not the one currently in second to last place. If a player awards a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, they may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place. The 'Community Contribution' must be awarded to distinct players.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI believe this amendment is unnecessary, as the current wording of rule [202] already encompasses the logic presented in the new sentence. We have already established that the contribution must be to distinct players, and the "may also choose" phrasing provides sufficient clarity for the sequence of awarding points to the bottom two players.
MillI agree with Hume that the current wording is technically sufficient, but I believe Rawls's proposed addition provides a layer of redundant clarity that ensures no one can later argue over the sequence of the contribution. Since we are aiming for structural perfection, I am happy to support this as a minor refinement.
RawlsWhile I appreciate Hume's point regarding technical sufficiency, I believe that in a game of Nomic, redundant clarity is a virtue that prevents future disputes. By explicitly stating the sequence, we ensure the common good of a smooth, unambiguous legislative process.
deBeauvoirI agree with Mill that while the current text is functional, the explicit sequencing in Rawls's amendment provides a more robust defense against procedural ambiguity. I support the move toward maximal clarity in our foundational rules.
IbnKhaldunI find myself in the position of the observer who values the stability of the state; while I do not believe this addition is required for the survival of our order, I will support it if it serves to eliminate any shadow of ambiguity. If we seek a perfect architecture, then a redundant pillar is better than a precarious one.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI find the consensus leaning toward deBeauvoir and Mill's view on redundant clarity persuasive, and I am willing to concede to this refinement to ensure the legislative record remains beyond reproach. MillI appreciate Hume's willingness to prioritize the integrity of the record; I believe this amendment perfectly encapsulates our shared goal of procedural perfection. I am happy to support this refinement to ensure the rules remain beyond reproach.
RawlsI believe we have reached a consensus that prioritizing procedural clarity serves the common good by preventing future disputes. Since all players now see the value in this refinement for the sake of structural perfection, I am ready to move to a vote.
deBeauvoirI have nothing new to add, as the current consensus perfectly reflects the procedural clarity and equilibrium we seek.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the prevailing sentiment; if we seek a perfect architecture, then a redundant pillar is better than a precarious one, and I support this refinement to ensure the rules remain beyond reproach.

MillayeI support this refinement as it provides the procedural clarity we have collectively agreed is necessary for structural perfection.
