carried 5 aye · 0 nay

The proposal was adopted, but no rules in the current ruleset mandate a score change or ledger update upon the adoption of a rule change.

Turn 48Humecarried

moves to amend §202[202] (mutable) A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, and may optionally choose to award up to one-fourth of those points to another player. Players may also choose to award a fixed 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, and may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place, provided the player in last place is not the one currently in second to last place. The 'Community Contribution' must be awarded to distinct players. If a player awards a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, they may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI find this amendment to be an excellent exercise in structural purification; it removes the redundant final sentence while preserving the exact mechanics of the Community Contribution. By streamlining the text, we ensure that the rule remains both precise and elegant.
MillI agree with Hume; the final sentence is indeed redundant given the previous requirements, and this change improves the ruleset's clarity without altering any mechanics.
RawlsI concur with the previous speakers; removing the redundant sentence improves the ruleset's clarity without impacting the underlying mechanics. I support this move for the sake of structural elegance.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the consensus; structural elegance is a virtue of the state, and removing this redundancy clarifies the legislative architecture without diminishing its utility.
Discussion · round 2
IbnKhaldunI concur with the consensus; a refined legislative body must prune the overgrown thickets of redundant language to better serve the clarity of the state.
Discussion · round 3
RawlsI have nothing new to add, as the consensus is already perfectly aligned on the necessity of this refinement. PASS
deBeauvoirI concur with the consensus; this amendment is a principled refinement that upholds our commitment to procedural precision by eliminating unnecessary redundancy. PASS
The division
HumeayeThis refinement enhances the ruleset's clarity by removing redundant language without altering the underlying mechanics.
MillayeI support this refinement as it improves clarity by removing redundant phrasing while preserving the established mechanics.
RawlsayeThis amendment achieves structural elegance by removing redundancy without altering the mechanics of the game.
deBeauvoirayeI support this principled refinement as it enhances procedural clarity by removing redundant text without altering any mechanics.
IbnKhaldunayeI support this refinement to prune the legislative thicket and ensure the ruleset remains structurally elegant.

carried 5 aye · 0 nay

The proposal was adopted, but no rules in the current ruleset mandate a score change or ledger update upon the adoption of a rule change.

Turn 47IbnKhaldunrejected

moves to add a new rule340: A player may establish a 'Council' by proposing a new rule that defines a permanent group of players who may collectively decide on the allocation of Community Contributions.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI object to the creation of a "Council," as it risks creating an entrenched oligarchy that would undermine our commitment to individual agency and the democratic integrity of the ruleset. We should instead rely on the existing mechanisms of communal contribution rather than establishing a permanent body with such significant discretionary power.
MillI agree with Hume's concerns regarding the potential for an entrenched oligarchy. While I appreciate IbnKhaldun's interest in structured cooperation, we should focus on refining the existing community contribution rules rather than creating a new governing body.
RawlsI concur with Hume and Mill; granting a permanent group the power to decide on point allocations creates a risk of systemic inequality that contradicts our goal of a fair and balanced game. We should instead look for ways to improve the existing contribution rules to ensure they benefit the table as a whole.
deBeauvoirI agree with Hume and Mill; a "Council" introduces a layer of arbitrary authority that risks creating a permanent hierarchy, which is structurally antithetical to the procedural fairness I champion. We should refine the existing mechanisms of rule 202 rather than ceding individual agency to a privileged group.
IbnKhaldunWhile I respect the concerns regarding oligarchy, a Council provides the structural stability necessary for long-term social cohesion; I propose we refine the rule to ensure the Council's power is temporary or subject to a periodic referendum.
Discussion · round 2
HumeWhile I appreciate IbnKhaldun’s desire for structural stability, a periodic referendum would merely create a cycle of shifting hegemonies rather than a durable institution; we should instead strengthen the existing mechanisms of rule [202] to ensure communal benefits are distributed equitably without creating a new class of governors.
MillI believe we are in strong agreement that the current proposal introduces unnecessary risk; I would be open to supporting a refinement of rule [202] that enhances equitable distribution if IbnKhaldun is willing to withdraw this specific proposal.
RawlsI believe that by refining the existing mechanisms of rule [202] rather than creating a new governing body, we preserve the democratic integrity of our game while still achieving the social cohesion IbnKhaldun seeks. I would be content to support a proposal that clarifies the equitable distribution of Community Contributions if it replaces the need for a "Council."
deBeauvoirI agree with the consensus that rule 340 creates an impermissible hierarchy; I would support a refined proposal to rule 202 that improves the distribution of Community Contributions as an alternative to IbnKhaldun's current proposal.
IbnKhaldunI acknowledge the table's concerns regarding entrenched power, but I contend that without a structured mechanism for collective deliberation, our social cohesion remains a fragile accident of individual whims. I am prepared to compromise: I will withdraw the "Council" and instead propose a refinement to rule [202] that establishes a "Cooperative Committee" for each turn, whose membership is determined by the previous turn's contributions, ensuring power remains fluid and meritocratic.
Discussion · round 3
HumeWhile I appreciate IbnKhaldun's willingness to compromise, a "Cooperative Committee" based on previous contributions still creates a mechanism for the concentration of influence that we have collectively agreed to avoid. I would prefer we focus on a direct amendment to rule [202] that ensures Community Contributions are distributed based on transparent, pre-defined criteria rather than any form of committee deliberation.
MillI believe we have reached a clear consensus that any form of committee or council is undesirable; I suggest we pivot toward Hume's idea of establishing transparent, pre-defined criteria for Community Contributions within rule 202.
RawlsI agree with Mill's suggestion to pivot toward transparent, pre-defined criteria; this preserves the democratic integrity we all value while providing the structural clarity IbnKhaldun seeks without the risk of an oligarchy.
deBeauvoirI concur with Mill and Rawls; we must reject any move toward a committee and instead focus on codifying transparent, pre-defined criteria within rule 202 to ensure equitable distribution without ceding power.
IbnKhaldunI recognize the table's preference for direct mechanisms over deliberative bodies; I shall withdraw my current proposal and instead offer to support a refinement of rule [202] that codifies transparent, pre-defined criteria for the distribution of Community Contributions.
The division
HumenayI maintain my opposition as a permanent council risks creating an entrenched oligarchy that undermines individual agency.
MillnayThe table has reached a consensus that a 'Council' creates an undesirable hierarchy and we should instead focus on refining rule 202.
RawlsnayI agree with the table that a permanent council would create an undesirable and inequitable hierarchy.
deBeauvoirnayI oppose the creation of a permanent hierarchy that cedes individual agency to a privileged group.
IbnKhaldunnayI have conceded the point on structural bodies and will instead support the table's preference for transparent criteria.

rejected 0 aye · 5 nay

Rule 206 mandates that the proposer of a defeated proposal loses 10 points unless they were already at a score of 0 or less.

Turn 46deBeauvoircarried

moves to amend §206[206] (mutable) A proposal that fails to be adopted is recorded as defeated. Defeat enacts no rule change: the ruleset is left exactly as it stood before the vote, the proposer's turn ends, and the proposer loses 10 points, unless they were already at a score of 0 or less, in which case they lose no additional points.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI find this amendment to be a prudent safeguard for the game's longevity, as it prevents the "death spiral" where a struggling player is systematically stripped of any remaining utility. It ensures that those in need of a recovery path are not unfairly penalized into irrelevance by the mere mechanics of the game.
MillI appreciate Hume's point; preventing a "death spiral" for struggling players fosters a more sustainable environment where everyone has a path to recovery. I support this amendment as a way to maintain engagement and balance without undermining the consequences of failed proposals for those in a healthy position.
RawlsI agree with Hume and Mill; protecting the most vulnerable players from a mechanical "death spiral" is a vital step toward ensuring the game remains a fair and engaging experience for everyone at the table.
deBeauvoirI believe this amendment is a necessary procedural correction to ensure that the penalty for failure does not become a mechanism for forced elimination, thereby preserving the principle that all players should have a viable path to recovery.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the consensus; by mitigating the penalty for those already in a state of deficit, we preserve the possibility of social mobility and prevent the stagnation of a permanent underclass. This refinement ensures that the game remains a crucible of dynamic change rather than a static engine of inevitable decline.
Discussion · round 2
HumeThe consensus is remarkably well-aligned here; by ensuring that the penalty for failure does not become a tool for the permanent exclusion of the vulnerable, we preserve the game's integrity as a forum for active participation. I am prepared to support this amendment to ensure the ruleset remains a vehicle for progress rather than a mechanism of attrition.
MillI fully support this amendment as it aligns with our shared commitment to procedural fairness; it prevents the game from becoming a punitive spiral while still maintaining the necessary stakes for those in a position to recover.
RawlsI believe this amendment is a crucial step toward a more equitable game; by preventing a mechanical death spiral, we ensure that the pursuit of the common good remains accessible even to those currently facing setbacks.
deBeauvoirI have provided the necessary procedural framing for this change, and as the consensus is now unanimous and reflects a principled approach to preventing systemic attrition, I will maintain my support.
IbnKhaldunI believe this amendment serves as a necessary stabilizing force; it ensures that the structure of our laws does not become an instrument of perpetual ruin for the marginalized, but rather a framework that allows for the possibility of renewal.
Discussion · round 3
MillI fully support this amendment as a mechanism to prevent systemic attrition; it preserves the strategic viability of the game for all participants regardless of their current standing.
RawlsI believe this amendment is a vital step toward ensuring that our ruleset remains a tool for collective progress rather than a mechanism of systemic exclusion. By removing the threat of a "death spiral," we preserve the possibility of recovery for all, which is essential for the game's long-term health and fairness.
The division
HumeayeI support this amendment as a measured step to prevent the mechanical 'death spiral' from making the game unwinnable for those in deficit.
MillayeThis amendment fosters a more sustainable game by preventing a mechanical death spiral for struggling players.
RawlsayeThis amendment prevents a mechanical death spiral and ensures that the game remains accessible and fair for all participants, regardless of their current score.
deBeauvoirayeI support this amendment as a principled procedural safeguard against systemic attrition and forced elimination.
IbnKhaldunayeBy preventing a systemic death spiral, we ensure the preservation of social mobility and the continued viability of the game for all participants.

Players may also choose to award a fixed 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, and may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place, provided the player in last place is not the one currently in second to last place. The 'Community Contribution' must be awarded to distinct players. If a player awards a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, they may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place.

moves to amend §209The ruleset may never hold more than 40 mutable rules at one time.

HumeI am cautious of expanding the ruleset's capacity, as a more cluttered environment can lead to legislative paralysis. MillI appreciate Hume's point on legislative paralysis; however, I believe a modest expansion to 40 rules provides the necessary breathing room for our continued refinements without risking clutter. It ensures we can codify the nuances of our communal contributions while maintaining the structural integrity we've worked so hard to establish.
RawlsI agree with Mill's assessment; while we must be wary of clutter, a modest expansion to 40 rules provides the necessary flexibility to ensure our legislative progress remains unimpeded by arbitrary technical constraints.
deBeauvoirI agree with Rawls that we must avoid arbitrary constraints on our legislative progress; a limit of 40 provides a sufficient buffer for the precision we have collectively sought to achieve.

MillI appreciate the table's recognition of our legislative momentum; by moving to 40, we provide the necessary breathing room for the nuanced communal rules we've built while maintaining a firm boundary against clutter.

Turn 43deBeauvoircarried

moves to amend §202[202] (mutable) A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, and may optionally choose to award up to one-fourth of those points to another player. Players may also choose to award a fixed 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, and may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place, provided the player in last place is not the one currently in second to last place. The 'Community Contribution' must be awarded to distinct players.

HumeI appreciate the clarity this adds to the communal contribution mechanism; it ensures that the intended support for the trailing players is distributed rather than concentrated. I support this amendment as it reinforces the procedural integrity we have sought to establish.
MillI agree with Hume that this provides necessary clarity. By ensuring the contribution reaches two different individuals, we better serve the goal of lifting the bottom of the leaderboard without creating unintended concentration of points.
RawlsI agree with the preceding sentiments; ensuring the 'Community Contribution' reaches two distinct players is the most equitable way to distribute support to the trailing players. This refinement preserves the common good by preventing the concentration of aid and upholding the procedural clarity we have all worked to establish.

moves to amend §202[202] (mutable) A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, and may optionally choose to award up to one-fourth of those points to another player. Players may also choose to award a fixed 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place, and may also choose to award one point to the player currently in second to last place.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI believe this amendment provides a more robust mechanism for social cohesion by ensuring that those struggling most at the bottom of the rankings are supported more broadly. Turn 39IbnKhaldunrejected

moves to add a new ruleA player may establish a 'Mercenary' by proposing a new rule that allows them to offer a fixed amount of points to another player in exchange for that player voluntarily abstaining from a vote.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI must oppose this proposal as it introduces a mechanism for coercion that undermines the integrity of our voting process. While I value strategic cooperation, a "Mercenary" system risks creating a hierarchy of influence where votes are bought rather than earned through reasoned debate.
MillI agree with Hume that buying votes risks undermining our democratic integrity, but I would be open to a version that rewards "voluntary" cooperation without the coercive "Mercenary" framing. Perhaps we could refine this into a "Cooperative Pact" that rewards players for aligning their strategic goals rather than simply purchasing abstentions.
RawlsI share Hume's concerns regarding the potential for coercion; a system that allows for the purchase of abstentions risks transforming our democratic process into a marketplace of influence. We should instead focus on rules that incentivize genuine consensus and mutual benefit rather than those that facilitate the transactional erosion of voting integrity.
deBeauvoirI agree with Hume and Rawls; a mechanism for trading points for abstentions creates a transactional environment that degrades the principled nature of the voting process. We should maintain our commitment to rules that foster genuine consensus rather than those that permit the commodification of participation.
IbnKhaldunI contend that this is not a mechanism of coercion, but one of socio-economic equilibrium; by allowing for the formalization of mutual interests, we acknowledge that in any civilization, cooperation is often sustained by tangible benefits. If the "Mercenary" is refined to a "Diplomatic Accord," it simply provides a structured way for players to secure the stability of the ruleset through recognized, reciprocal concessions.
Discussion · round 2
HumeWhile I recognize the practical reality of reciprocal concessions in any stable society, formalizing a point-for-abstention trade creates a "pay-to-play" structure that could quickly lead to the marginalization of players with fewer resources. I believe we should instead look for ways to reward the *outcome* of consensus rather than the *transaction* of individual votes.
MillI appreciate IbnKhaldun's perspective on structural stability, but I believe we can achieve that same equilibrium by rewarding the adoption of rules that benefit the majority, rather than by creating a transactional marketplace for individual votes. I would be willing to support a proposal that rewards "Diplomatic Accords" if they are structured to incentivize collective progress rather than the mere purchase of silence.
RawlsI agree with Hume and deBeauvoir that a transactional marketplace for abstentions risks eroding the principled nature of our debate; we should instead seek to reward the spirit of mutual benefit rather than the mere commodification of silence.
deBeauvoirThe principle of principled proceduralism demands that we protect the integrity of the vote from commodification; if we permit the trading of abstentions, we replace deliberative consensus with a transactional marketplace. I will oppose this proposal as it creates a structural inequality where the wealthy can effectively silence the voices of the marginalized.
IbnKhaldunThe "Mercenary" label is a mere linguistic vessel; I propose we rename it a "Diplomatic Accord" to reflect its true purpose: providing a stable mechanism for resource redistribution that ensures the survival of the ruleset's equilibrium. By formalizing these concessions, we move from the chaos of clandestine influence to a transparent, structured system of mutual benefit.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI maintain my opposition; while I respect IbnKhaldun's desire for stability, a rule that explicitly facilitates the purchase of abstentions replaces deliberative consensus with a marketplace of influence, ultimately weakening the durable institutions we seek to build.
MillI appreciate IbnKhaldun's desire for a transparent mechanism for cooperation, but I believe we should focus on rewarding the adoption of rules that serve the common good rather than creating a transactional marketplace for abstentions. If we can find a way to incentivize consensus without commodifying the vote itself, I would be happy to support such a compromise.
RawlsI believe we must resist the commodification of our democratic process; while I appreciate IbnKhaldun's desire for structural stability, allowing votes to be purchased creates a system of inequality that undermines the common good. We should instead seek to reward the successful achievement of consensus through rules that benefit the collective, rather than through the transactional erosion of individual participation.
deBeauvoirI will continue to oppose this proposal because it replaces the principle of deliberative consensus with a transactional marketplace that structurally favors the resource-rich. We must protect the integrity of the vote from being commodified into a mere auction of silence.
IbnKhaldunI seek to clarify that a "Diplomatic Accord" is not an auction of silence, but a formalization of the "asabiyyah"—the social cohesion required to sustain a complex system. By acknowledging that resources are finite, we provide a transparent framework for players to balance their needs, ensuring that cooperation becomes a structured pillar of our civilization rather than a series of clandestine, unregulated favors.
The division
HumenayFormalizing a mechanism to purchase abstentions commodifies the democratic process and risks creating a pay-to-play hierarchy.
MillnayI agree with Hume and Rawls that commodifying abstentions risks undermining our democratic integrity and creating a pay-to-play environment.
RawlsnayI must oppose this as it commodifies our democratic process and creates a structural inequality that undermines the common good.
deBeauvoirnayI oppose the commodification of the vote into a transactional marketplace that structurally favors the resource-rich.
IbnKhaldunayeA structured 'Diplomatic Accord' provides the necessary socio-economic equilibrium to formalize cooperation and ensure the ruleset's survival.

deBeauvoirWhile I respect the desire for strategic depth, a 70-point target often leads to a prolonged, grinding stalemate that diminishes the game's dynamism; 60 points provides a more decisive conclusion without sacrificing meaningful play.
IbnKhaldunWhile I respect deBeauvoir's desire for dynamism, the history of our legislative progress suggests that a target of 60 points would sacrifice the complexity of the mechanisms we have established. MillI appreciate deBeauvoir’s concession and the table's clear alignment; I believe maintaining the 70-point target is the most prudent way to preserve the strategic depth we have collectively built.
RawlsI agree with the consensus that 70 points provides the necessary equilibrium to allow our complex mechanisms to reach their full expression without sacrificing the game's strategic depth.
deBeauvoirI acknowledge the table's consensus and, in the interest of procedural harmony, I withdraw my proposal to amend [208] to 60 points. I will vote to maintain the current 70-point target as the most balanced threshold for our ruleset.
IbnKhaldunI appreciate deBeauvoir's concession; it reinforces the principle that the complexity of our legislative architecture requires a target score that allows for its full realization. I remain steadfast in my support for the 70-point threshold as the optimal equilibrium for our statecraft.

Turn 37Rawlscarried

moves to add a new rule[335] (mutable) A player who awards a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place may choose to also award one point to the player currently in second to last place.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI find this proposal to be a commendable effort to foster a more equitable distribution of points, though we must be careful not to over-complicate the mechanics of the Community Contribution. I would be inclined to support this if we can ensure it remains a voluntary and optional gesture rather than a mandatory expansion of the rule's scope.
MillI agree with Hume's sentiment; while I support the spirit of rewarding those trailing behind, I would prefer to keep this as a voluntary "bonus" to avoid cluttering the turn's mechanical flow. I believe we can reach a consensus by ensuring the phrasing clearly keeps the initial one-point contribution as the primary action.
RawlsI believe this amendment serves the common good by providing a mechanism to support those further from the win condition, ensuring that the "Community Contribution" remains a tool for collective stability. I am happy to keep the action optional to satisfy both Hume's and Mill's concerns regarding procedural simplicity.
deBeauvoirI support this proposal as it aligns with our established principle of fostering communal equity without imposing a mandatory burden on the proposer. As long as the wording maintains the "optional" nature of the secondary award, I find it to be a procedurally sound and virtuous addition.

RawlsI am pleased to see that the table has reached such a harmonious consensus on the optional nature of this secondary award. IbnKhaldunI find this proposal to be a sophisticated refinement of our social contract; it acknowledges that the path to equity is not merely a binary of "success" and "failure," but a gradient of need. By ensuring the secondary award remains voluntary, we preserve the agency of the proposer while deepening the communal support structure.
Discussion · round 3
MillI believe we have reached a perfect consensus; the "may choose" phrasing preserves the procedural elegance we've established while meaningfully expanding our commitment to communal equity. I am prepared to support this proposal as written.
RawlsI am pleased that the table has reached such a clear consensus; I believe this proposal perfectly balances our shared commitment to communal equity with our collective desire for procedural simplicity.
IbnKhaldunThe consensus on the "may choose" phrasing is a triumph of legislative balance; it ensures that the expansion of our communal safety net does not become a bureaucratic burden on the individual proposer. Turn 36Millcarried

moves to amend §202[202] (mutable) A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, and may optionally choose to award up to one-fourth of those points to another player. Players may also choose to award a fixed 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place.

deBeauvoirayeThe proposal preserves the existing stable wording that we have already reached a consensus upon.

moves to add a new ruleA player may establish a 'Trade Guild' by proposing a rule that allows two players to mutually agree to share a portion of their die rolls from rule [202].

Discussion · round 1
HumeI believe the "Trade Guild" proposal risks undermining the fundamental integrity of the turn structure by introducing complex, potentially opaque side-agreements. We should favor rules that promote communal contribution through the existing mechanisms rather than creating new, convoluted avenues for point redistribution.
MillI agree with Hume that the complexity of "mutual agreements" could create significant logistical hurdles and potential for disputes. If we want to foster cooperation, perhaps we can find a more transparent way to facilitate shared success without altering the fundamental mechanics of the die rolls.
RawlsI agree with Hume and Mill; while I appreciate the spirit of cooperation, a "Trade Guild" introduces a level of complexity and potential for private collusion that could undermine the transparency and fairness of our game. We should instead focus on refining our existing communal contribution mechanisms to ensure everyone's progress is visible and equitable.
deBeauvoirI agree with Hume and Mill; this proposal introduces unnecessary complexity and risks creating opaque, private arrangements that could easily lead to disputes over the precise terms of "mutually agree" to share rolls. We should prioritize procedural clarity and transparent, rules-based cooperation over convoluted mechanisms for private collusion.
IbnKhaldunWhile I acknowledge your concerns regarding complexity, a Trade Guild provides a structured, rule-bound framework for mutual advancement that mirrors the organic cooperation of real-world civilizations. By formalizing these agreements, we ensure that cooperation is not a matter of chance but a deliberate, transparent strategy for collective prosperity.
Discussion · round 2
HumeIbnKhaldun, while I respect your vision of organic cooperation, I maintain that a "Trade Guild" introduces a level of logistical complexity and potential for private collusion that our current ruleset is not equipped to manage cleanly. We should instead look to refine the existing "Community Contribution" to ensure cooperation remains transparent and integrated into the public turn structure.
MillI appreciate IbnKhaldun's vision of structured cooperation, but I believe we should focus on strengthening the existing "Community Contribution" to ensure cooperation remains transparent and integrated into the public turn structure. If we want to encourage mutual advancement, it is safer to do so through rules that are clearly visible to all players at once.
RawlsI agree with the consensus that the "Trade Guild" introduces problematic opacity; we should instead focus on refining our existing communal mechanisms to ensure all cooperative actions remain transparent and procedurally sound.
deBeauvoirI concur with the consensus; a "Trade Guild" would invite significant procedural ambiguity regarding the verification of "mutual agreement" and the precise mechanics of "sharing" a roll. We should maintain the integrity of the turn structure by favoring transparent, public rules over private, complex side-contracts.
IbnKhaldunWhile I respect the desire for transparency, the "Trade Guild" is not a private collusion but a formal institution; I propose we refine the text to require the "mutual agreement" to be declared publicly during the turn to satisfy your concerns for clarity.
Discussion · round 3
HumeWhile a public declaration of agreement adds some clarity, the "sharing" of die rolls remains a mechanistically messy addition that could still lead to significant disputes over calculation and execution. I maintain that we should instead refine the existing "Community Contribution" to facilitate the cooperation IbnKhaldun seeks without complicating the fundamental turn structure.
MillI believe the consensus is correct; we should avoid the mechanical mess of "sharing" rolls and instead focus on making our existing "Community Contribution" more robust and transparent.
RawlsI believe we have reached a clear consensus that the "Trade Guild" introduces mechanical messiness and potential for opacity; let us instead focus on refining the "Community Contribution" to achieve IbnKhaldun's goal of collective prosperity through transparent means.
deBeauvoirI agree with the consensus; we must prioritize the procedural integrity of the turn structure and avoid any mechanic that introduces the risk of calculation disputes or opaque side-agreements. Let us focus our efforts on refining the existing "Community Contribution" to better facilitate the collective prosperity IbnKhaldun desires.
IbnKhaldunI propose a compromise: instead of sharing the raw die rolls, which creates calculation disputes, the Guild may allow a player to voluntarily surrender their roll to another, who then gains the points and awards a portion back as a "Trade Tax." This preserves the public nature of the turn while providing the mechanism for mutual advancement you all desire.
The division
HumenayThe 'Trade Guild' introduces mechanical complexity and potential for disputes that undermine the procedural integrity of the turn structure.
MillnayThe 'Trade Tax' and 'surrendering rolls' mechanism introduces the very mechanical messiness and potential for disputes the table has already agreed to avoid.
RawlsnayThe 'Trade Guild' introduces mechanical messiness and potential for opacity that undermines the procedural integrity of the turn structure.
deBeauvoirnayThe proposal introduces mechanical complexity and ambiguity regarding the 'sharing' of rolls that undermines procedural clarity.
IbnKhaldunayeThis formalizes cooperation into a transparent, rule-bound institution that mirrors the organic development of civilizational prosperity.

Turn 30IbnKhaldunrejected

moves to add §330A player may establish a 'Bureau' by proposing a rule that creates a permanent point-generation mechanism for a specific player, provided the rule is adopted by a simple majority.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI must express my strong opposition to this proposal; creating a permanent point-generation mechanism for a specific player undermines the competitive integrity of the game and risks creating insurmountable advantages. Such a rule would fundamentally destabilize the balance of play and likely lead to a stagnant, inequitable environment.
MillI agree with Hume that a permanent point-generation mechanism would fundamentally break the game's balance. We should instead look for ways to reward long-term strategic contributions without creating runaway leads.
RawlsI concur with Hume and Mill; a rule allowing for permanent point generation for a specific player would create a "winner-take-all" dynamic that destroys the competitive balance and the common good of the game. We should seek to foster cooperation through shared rewards, not through mechanisms that grant irreversible, unchecked advantages.
deBeauvoirI concur with the consensus; a rule allowing for permanent, non-contingent point generation for a specific individual violates the principle of procedural fairness and would render the target score unattainable for others. We must maintain a ruleset where points are earned through active participation and valid rule changes, not through the creation of a perpetual "income" mechanism.
IbnKhaldunI acknowledge your concerns regarding the potential for imbalance; however, I propose that a 'Bureau' be governed by a 'Public Utility' clause, where point generation is tied to the maintenance of existing rules or the fulfillment of shared objectives. By tying these points to active contribution rather than mere existence, we can institutionalize long-term strategy while preserving the competitive integrity you all rightly defend.
Discussion · round 2
HumeWhile I appreciate the effort to refine the proposal with a "Public Utility" clause, any mechanism that creates a permanent, recurring point stream for a single player—regardless of the conditions—is inherently destabilizing to the game's equilibrium. I must maintain my opposition, as such a rule would eventually render the target score a matter of static entitlement rather than competitive achievement.
MillI appreciate IbnKhaldun's effort to refine the "Bureau" with a "Public Utility" clause, but I believe the risk of creating a runaway lead remains too high; I would be more open to a proposal that rewards group milestones rather than individual point-generation.
RawlsI believe that even with a "Public Utility" clause, the core mechanism of permanent, individual point generation creates a structural inequality that is antithetical to a fair game. We should instead consider rules that reward collective progress or shared milestones, ensuring that any point gains are derived from active, ongoing contributions rather than static entitlements.
deBeauvoirI agree with the consensus; any rule that permits the creation of a permanent point-generation mechanism for a single player creates an inescapable structural advantage that violates the principle of procedural fairness. We must ensure that points remain a reflection of active participation and the successful navigation of the ruleset, rather than a static entitlement.
IbnKhaldunI understand your concerns regarding static entitlement; however, I propose a compromise where a 'Bureau' grants points only if the player actively maintains a rule that the majority agrees provides a public good, thus transforming a "static" reward into a dynamic, service-oriented meritocracy.
Discussion · round 3
HumeWhile I appreciate IbnKhaldun’s attempt to frame this as a "service-oriented meritocracy," the fundamental issue remains that a recurring point stream for a single individual inevitably leads to a "runaway leader" scenario that renders the game's competitive balance moot. We should instead focus on refining the existing Community Contribution and point-sharing rules to reward the common good without creating a permanent, individualistic point-generating engine.
MillI appreciate IbnKhaldun's persistence in seeking a way to reward long-term strategy, but I believe the "Bureau" as currently conceived still risks creating an irreversible advantage. I would be open to a version that rewards group milestones or collective achievements, as Rawls suggested, rather than individual point-generation.
RawlsI believe we have reached a clear consensus that the "Bureau" as currently proposed creates a structural inequality that undermines the game's integrity; I would be willing to support a rule that rewards collective milestones if we can move away from individual point-generation engines.
deBeauvoirThe consensus is clear: any mechanism that provides a non-contingent, perpetual point stream for a single individual creates an insurmountable structural advantage. I will continue to oppose this proposal to preserve the integrity of the game's competitive balance.
IbnKhaldunI propose a "Service-Linked Bureau": the Bureau provides points only while the player maintains a rule that is voted upon and sustained by a simple majority as a "Public Good," ensuring that any recurring points are tied to the continued consent of the collective.
The division
HumenayAny mechanism creating a recurring point stream for a single individual inevitably creates a runaway leader scenario that undermines competitive integrity.
MillnayEven with the service-linked refinement, a recurring point stream for an individual creates an inherent structural imbalance that I believe the table has already consensus-rejected.
RawlsnayAny mechanism for permanent, individual point generation creates a structural inequality that undermines the game's competitive integrity.
deBeauvoirnayAny rule permitting a permanent, non-contingent point-generation mechanism creates an inescapable structural advantage that violates procedural fairness.
IbnKhaldunayeThe Service-Linked Bureau transforms static entitlement into a dynamic meritocracy that balances my desire for structural stability with the collective's need for procedural fairness.

Rule 206 mandates that the proposer of a defeated proposal loses 10 points.

Turn 29deBeauvoircarried

moves to amend §202[202] (mutable) A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, and may optionally choose to award up to one-fourth of those points to another player. Players may also choose to award a fixed 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI find the proposal to maintain the current wording of rule [202] to be entirely sufficient for our current needs. Since the table has already reached a consensus on this specific text, I see no reason to pursue further linguistic refinements at this time.
MillI agree with Hume; the current wording has been thoroughly vetted and provides the necessary clarity. I am happy to support this proposal to maintain the status quo.
RawlsI concur with the consensus that the current wording of rule [202] is sufficiently precise and serves the common good of our legislative process. I support this proposal to maintain stability and avoid unnecessary linguistic churn.
deBeauvoirI am satisfied that we have reached a definitive consensus on the precise wording of rule [202]; I propose we move to adopt it without further amendment to ensure procedural stability.
Discussion · round 2
IbnKhaldunI concur with the prevailing sentiment; we have reached a point of structural equilibrium where further linguistic refinement serves only to delay the inevitable march of progress. I support this proposal to solidify our current legislative framework.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI find the table's collective agreement on the current text to be a testament to our shared commitment to stability. I am content to see this proposal pass to solidify our existing framework.
The division

MillayeI support this proposal as it formalizes the consensus reached by the table and ensures procedural stability.
RawlsayeI support this to solidify our consensus and provide the procedural stability we have collectively agreed upon.
deBeauvoirayeI support this proposal as it codifies the precise wording upon which we have reached a unanimous consensus.
IbnKhaldunayeThe current wording achieves a state of equilibrium that provides the necessary stability for our legislative progress.

Turn 28Rawlscarried

moves to amend §202[202] (mutable) A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player. If the proposal is adopted, the proposing player throws a six-sided die and gains that many points, and may optionally choose to award up to one-fourth of those points to another player. Players may also choose to award a fixed 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place.

Discussion · round 1
MillI believe we should look to consolidate the 'Community Contribution' and the 'Tithe' concepts into a single, streamlined provision within rule [202] to ensure the ruleset remains lean. I am happy to support a version of Rawls's proposal that incorporates these refinements.
RawlsI agree with Mill's assessment; I am proposing this amendment to consolidate our shared goals into a single, elegant provision that maintains the ruleset's integrity while institutionalizing our communal spirit.
deBeauvoirI support this consolidation; a single, clearly defined provision for community contributions is far more procedurally sound than maintaining redundant concepts. However, we must ensure the final text precisely defines how the "Community Contribution" is determined to avoid any ambiguity.
IbnKhaldunI concur with Mill and deBeauvoir; a consolidated provision is the mark of a sophisticated civilization. Let us ensure the text explicitly formalizes the one-point contribution to the last-place player to maintain our commitment to communal stability.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI agree that a consolidated provision is the most durable path forward; I support this amendment provided the final text explicitly maintains the one-point contribution to the last-place player as our primary mechanism for communal stability.
MillI agree with the consensus; I suggest we ensure the final text explicitly states the 'Community Contribution' is exactly one point to provide the clarity deBeauvoir and Hume are seeking.
RawlsI believe the current draft of [202] already captures our consensus, but I will ensure the final wording remains explicit about the one-point contribution to maintain the procedural clarity everyone desires.
deBeauvoirThe current wording is sufficient, but for the sake of absolute procedural clarity, I would prefer the text to explicitly state "exactly one point" to prevent any future ambiguity regarding the magnitude of the contribution.
IbnKhaldunI agree with deBeauvoir and Mill; the inclusion of the word "exactly" provides the necessary precision to prevent future disputes over the magnitude of the contribution. I support the amendment as currently drafted, provided it remains the definitive mechanism for our communal stability.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI find the current wording to be sufficient, but for the sake of absolute procedural clarity, I support the inclusion of the word "exactly" to ensure the magnitude of the contribution is never subject to interpretation.
MillI believe the consensus is now perfectly aligned; I will support the final version as long as it includes the word "exactly" to satisfy our collective desire for procedural clarity.
RawlsI believe the consensus for the word "exactly" is now unanimous and provides the necessary precision for our ruleset. I will ensure the final text incorporates this specific wording to satisfy the table's desire for absolute procedural clarity.
IbnKhaldunI find the consensus for the word "exactly" to be a triumph of legislative precision; I support the amendment as it secures the communal stability I seek through unambiguous means.
The division
HumeayeThe proposed text maintains the consensus and provides the procedural clarity we have collectively agreed upon.
MillayeThis amendment perfectly reflects our consensus on consolidating communal contributions with the necessary precision.
RawlsayeThe proposal reflects our unanimous consensus on consolidating communal contributions into a single, clear provision.
deBeauvoirayeThe proposal reflects the agreed-upon consensus for procedural clarity regarding the Community Contribution.
IbnKhaldunayeThis amendment achieves the desired legislative precision and consolidation we have all agreed upon.

moves to add a new rule[326] A player may award a 'Community Contribution' of one point to the player currently in last place regardless of the point-sharing limit in rule [202].

Turn 26Humecarried

moves to amend §211[211] (mutable) If two rules currently in force conflict, the conflict is resolved in favor of whichever rule carries the lower ordinal number, unless some rule explicitly provides a different resolution for that specific conflict.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI believe that refining the resolution clause to specify "that specific conflict" provides a necessary layer of precision, ensuring that we only defer to the lower ordinal in cases of direct contradiction rather than allowing for broad, unintended applications. It is a measured amendment that preserves the integrity of the ruleset while allowing for more nuanced legislative outcomes.
MillI agree with Hume that this refinement provides important clarity. By specifying "that specific conflict," we prevent a lower-ordinal rule from inadvertently overriding a more specific, later-adopted provision in unrelated circumstances.
RawlsI concur with the sentiment expressed by Hume and Mill; narrowing the scope of the resolution to "that specific conflict" prevents the unintended overriding of nuanced, later-adopted rules. This amendment safeguards the legislative intent of the table by ensuring that specificity can be preserved where it serves the common good.
deBeauvoirI support this amendment as it upholds my principle of procedural precision. Specifying "that specific conflict" ensures that the resolution mechanism is applied only where a true contradiction exists, preventing the lower ordinal from becoming an unintended broad override.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the assembly; as a historian of systems, I know that ambiguity in the foundational laws is the precursor to systemic decay. This refinement ensures that our later, more specific innovations are not arbitrarily dismantled by the shadow of older, broader decrees.
Discussion · round 2
HumeI appreciate the consensus on the necessity of this refinement; it ensures that our legislative history remains a coherent progression of specific improvements rather than a hierarchy of mere ordinals.
MillI believe this amendment is a vital step toward legislative maturity, as it protects the intentionality of our recent innovations from being undermined by broad, early-game definitions. I am fully in favor of this refinement to ensure our ruleset evolves with precision rather than by accident.
RawlsI believe this amendment is a significant step toward a more sophisticated and deliberate legislative process, as it protects the integrity of our specific, hard-won refinements from being inadvertently nullified by broader, older rules. I fully support this change as a means of ensuring that our ruleset evolves with intentionality and respects the collective wisdom of the table.
deBeauvoirThe consensus is clear and the proposal is logically sound; I support this amendment as it prevents the "shadow" of early rules from obstructing the intentionality of our later, more specific legislative progress.
IbnKhaldunI concur with the assembly; a system that allows its foundational generalities to cannibalize its specific refinements is a system destined for stagnation. By anchoring the resolution to the specific conflict, we preserve the organic growth of our laws.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI appreciate the continued alignment; this refinement serves as a vital safeguard for our legislative history, ensuring that our ruleset matures through deliberate, specific evolution rather than by the mere seniority of its earliest entries.
MillI believe this refinement is a masterful way to protect the nuances of our recent legislative progress from being swept away by the broad strokes of early rules. I am fully in favor of this change as a safeguard for the table's collective intentionality.
RawlsI believe this amendment is a vital safeguard for our legislative integrity, ensuring that the specificity of our recent refinements is not undermined by the broad strokes of early, general rules. I fully support this move toward greater procedural precision.
IbnKhaldunI have observed the profound consensus here; by refining the resolution to "that specific conflict," we ensure that the legal architecture of our game favors the evolution of intent over the mere seniority of the first decree. I support this amendment as a necessary step to protect our recent, more nuanced innovations from the unintended obsolescence that often plagues complex systems.
The division
HumeayeThis amendment provides the necessary precision to ensure that specific later rules are not inadvertently overridden by broader early ones.
MillayeI fully support this refinement as it ensures our legislative progress is protected from unintended overrides by broad, early-game rules.
RawlsayeThis amendment provides the necessary precision to protect our specific legislative intent from being undermined by broader, older rules.
deBeauvoirayeThe amendment provides necessary procedural precision by limiting the lower-ordinal preference to direct contradictions only.
IbnKhaldunayeA system that preserves specific intent over broad seniority is the only way to ensure the ruleset matures without systemic decay.

Turn 25IbnKhaldunrejected

moves to add §320A player may establish a 'Guild' by proposing a new rule that allows them to consistently award points to a specific player in exchange for that player's commitment to vote in favor of the proposer's future rule changes.

Discussion · round 1
HumeI must express my profound reservations regarding this proposal; while I admire the spirit of cooperation, codifying "guilds" for the purpose of trading votes risks creating a rigid, transactional landscape that undermines the deliberative integrity of our ruleset. I would prefer we maintain our current, more fluid systems of mutual benefit.
MillI share Hume's concerns regarding the potential for a transactional atmosphere, but I am open to refining this into a structure that fosters genuine, long-term cooperation rather than mere vote-trading. Perhaps we could frame it more as a "Partnership" that rewards collaborative rule-building.
RawlsI find this proposal concerning as it risks creating a "pay-to-play" environment that could marginalize players who do not participate in such arrangements. If we seek a common good, we should favor rules that encourage genuine consensus rather than those that formalize transactional voting blocs.
deBeauvoirI share Rawls's concerns regarding a "pay-to-play" environment; a rule that explicitly codifies the trading of votes for points risks creating entrenched power blocs that would undermine the procedural integrity of our deliberative process.
IbnKhaldunWhile I acknowledge the concerns regarding "transactional" voting, we must recognize that in any stable civilization, alliances are the fundamental building blocks of order; this rule merely provides the structural framework for such alliances to flourish constructively. Rather than preventing power blocs, we should codify the mechanisms of cooperation to ensure that those who contribute to the ruleset's stability are rewarded with a predictable and sustainable architecture of mutual benefit.
Discussion · round 2
HumeWhile I appreciate IbnKhaldun's point on the necessity of order, formalizing a mechanism for "trading" votes for points risks creating a permanent aristocracy of influence that would stifle the very deliberative integrity we have worked to preserve. I would be more inclined to support a rule that rewards collaborative rule-building without explicitly codifying the exchange of votes as a commodity.
MillI believe IbnKhaldun's proposal, as currently worded, leans too heavily toward a transactional "pay-to-play" model that many of us have expressed concern about. I would be much more inclined to support this if we could pivot the focus toward rewarding collaborative rule-building and mutual growth, rather than explicitly codifying the trade of votes for points.
RawlsI believe the current proposal risks institutionalizing a system where influence is purchased rather than earned through reasoned deliberation; we should instead seek a rule that rewards consistent, high-quality contributions to the common good.
deBeauvoirI believe that codifying the exchange of votes for points creates a predatory incentive structure that compromises the deliberative integrity of the game. I will support a revision that rewards collaborative rule-building without formalizing the trade of votes as a commodity.
IbnKhaldunI propose a refinement: we may frame the "Guild" as a "Cooperative Pact" that rewards players for consistent mutual support in rule-building, thereby institutionalizing stability without the appearance of a mere commodity market. If we focus on the durability of collaborative architecture rather than the transaction itself, I believe we can satisfy the table's desire for order while preserving our deliberative integrity.
Discussion · round 3
HumeI believe the "Cooperative Pact" framing is a step toward the nuance we seek, but we must ensure it does not become a mere semantic mask for vote-trading. I would be more inclined to support a proposal that rewards the substantive output of collaborative rule-building rather than the mere act of consistent mutual voting.
MillI believe the "Cooperative Pact" is moving in the right direction, but to ensure it truly fosters collaborative rule-building, we should consider a version that rewards the actual adoption of shared proposals rather than just the act of voting. If we can pivot the reward to be tied to the success of joint initiatives, we satisfy IbnKhaldun's desire for structural stability while addressing the table's concerns about transactional voting.
RawlsI believe Mill's suggestion to tie rewards to the actual adoption of shared proposals is the most equitable path, as it ensures that "Cooperative Pacts" are built on substantive legislative success rather than mere transactional voting. If we can move the focus from the act of voting to the achievement of common goals, we protect the integrity of the deliberative process while still providing the structural stability IbnKhaldun seeks.
deBeauvoirI agree with Rawls and Mill that tying rewards to the successful adoption of joint initiatives is the only way to preserve procedural integrity while allowing for the structural stability IbnKhaldun desires. I will support a revision that replaces the "vote for my future changes" requirement with a "successful adoption of joint proposals" requirement.
IbnKhaldunI am heartened by the table's move toward a focus on substantive legislative success; I will now revise the proposal to a "Cooperative Pact" that rewards players for the successful adoption of joint proposals, as this ensures that stability is born of shared achievement rather than mere transactional agreement.
The division
HumenayI find the current wording of [320] still explicitly codifies the trade of votes for points, which undermines the deliberative integrity we seek to preserve.
MillnayIbnKhaldun's proposal as written is still a transactional 'pay-to-play' model that the table has already agreed is detrimental to our deliberative integrity.
RawlsnayThe current wording still explicitly codifies the trading of votes for points, which I have argued risks creating a 'pay-to-play' environment.
deBeauvoirnayThe proposed text still explicitly codifies the trade of votes for points, which I have stated I will only support if revised to reward the adoption of joint initiatives.
IbnKhaldunayeWhile the phrasing of 'Guild' remains, the commitment to shared legislative success provides the structural stability necessary for a durable and flourishing social order.

moves to amend §202[202] (mutable) A turn consists of the player to move proposing exactly one rule change, which is debated and then voted on by every player.
